metadata.description: Standard normal examination results for children.

results.male_result: Inspection: In infants and toddlers, the abdomen is normally protuberant ('pot-bellied') both supine and standing due to weak abdominal musculature; it becomes flatter appearing in school-aged children. Respiration is primarily abdominal until approximately 6-7 years of age. Umbilicus is central; mild umbilical hernias are common in infants but usually resolve. No visible peristalsis or distended veins. Auscultation: Bowel sounds are normoactive. Percussion: Generally tympanic (often more pronounced in infants due to air swallowing); dullness over liver and spleen. Palpation: Abdomen is soft and non-tender. The liver edge is normally palpable 1-2 cm below the right costal margin in infants and young children. The spleen tip may be palpable 1-2 cm below the left costal margin in a small percentage of healthy young children. Kidneys may be palpable in neonates. No inguinal hernias.
results.female_result: Inspection: In infants and toddlers, the abdomen is normally protuberant ('pot-bellied') both supine and standing due to weak abdominal musculature; it becomes flatter appearing in school-aged children. Respiration is primarily abdominal until approximately 6-7 years of age. Umbilicus is central; mild umbilical hernias are common in infants but usually resolve. No visible peristalsis or distended veins. Auscultation: Bowel sounds are normoactive. Percussion: Generally tympanic (often more pronounced in infants due to air swallowing); dullness over liver and spleen. Palpation: Abdomen is soft and non-tender. The liver edge is normally palpable 1-2 cm below the right costal margin in infants and young children. The spleen tip may be palpable 1-2 cm below the left costal margin in a small percentage of healthy young children. Kidneys may be palpable in neonates. No inguinal hernias.

results.male_result: Not applicable - This is an emergency intervention technique used to relieve foreign body airway obstruction, not a diagnostic examination. In healthy male children, no airway obstruction is present, therefore this maneuver is not performed as a routine examination.
results.female_result: Not applicable - This is an emergency intervention technique used to relieve foreign body airway obstruction, not a diagnostic examination. In healthy female children, no airway obstruction is present, therefore this maneuver is not performed as a routine examination.

results.male_result: Negative. With the patient supine and the knee flexed to 90 degrees, there is no pathological anterior translation of the tibia relative to the femur. While children frequently demonstrate greater baseline physiological laxity (hypermobility) than adults, the translation must be symmetric when compared to the uninjured contralateral knee and must exhibit a firm endpoint. No pain or instability is elicited.
results.female_result: Negative. With the patient supine and the knee flexed to 90 degrees, there is no pathological anterior translation of the tibia relative to the femur. While children frequently demonstrate greater baseline physiological laxity (hypermobility) than adults, the translation must be symmetric when compared to the uninjured contralateral knee and must exhibit a firm endpoint. No pain or instability is elicited.

results.male_result: Nasal vestibule is intact; vibrissae are typically sparse or fine compared to adults. Nasal septum is midline without deviation, perforation, or hematoma. Inferior and middle turbinates are proportionate in size to the nasal cavity and pink in color. Nasal mucosa is pink, smooth, and moist. Nasal passages are patent bilaterally with no obstruction. No foreign bodies, polyps, masses, or purulent discharge observed.
results.female_result: Nasal vestibule is intact; vibrissae are typically sparse or fine compared to adults. Nasal septum is midline without deviation, perforation, or hematoma. Inferior and middle turbinates are proportionate in size to the nasal cavity and pink in color. Nasal mucosa is pink, smooth, and moist. Nasal passages are patent bilaterally with no obstruction. No foreign bodies, polyps, masses, or purulent discharge observed.

results.male_result: Highly age-dependent; evaluated against standard growth charts (e.g., WHO or CDC). Weight and Height: Normal range falls between the 3rd and 97th percentiles (or -2 to +2 Z-scores) for specific age. BMI (ages 2-19): 5th to <85th percentile is considered healthy weight. Head Circumference: Approx. 34 cm at birth, reaching ~46 cm by age 1; critical to monitor in early childhood. MUAC: >12.5 cm (for ages 6-59 months).
results.female_result: Highly age-dependent; evaluated against sex-specific growth charts. Weight and Height: Normal range falls between the 3rd and 97th percentiles for specific age. Girls typically enter the pubertal growth spurt earlier than boys. BMI (ages 2-19): 5th to <85th percentile is considered healthy weight. Head Circumference: Slightly smaller than males on average (approx. 33.5 cm at birth). MUAC: >12.5 cm (for ages 6-59 months).

results.male_result: Normal score: 7-10 (Applicable only to newborns)
results.female_result: Normal score: 7-10 (Applicable only to newborns)

results.male_result: Negative - No palpable lymph nodes at the medial aspect of the inguinal ligament (Cloquet's node area). The region is soft, non-tender. (Note: Small, mobile, non-tender superficial inguinal lymph nodes are frequently palpable in healthy children, but the deep Cloquet's node should not be palpable).
results.female_result: Negative - No palpable lymph nodes at the medial aspect of the inguinal ligament (Cloquet's node area). The region is soft, non-tender. (Note: Small, mobile, non-tender superficial inguinal lymph nodes are frequently palpable in healthy children, but the deep Cloquet's node should not be palpable).

results.male_result: Negative. The test is performed with the child seated or supine. The examiner abducts the arm, extends the elbow, and performs nerve-specific biasing maneuvers (like wrist extension and neck lateral flexion). Normal finding: No reproduction of radicular pain, paresthesia, or abnormal neurological symptoms. The child should not exhibit protective guarding or behavioral signs of distress (e.g., grimacing or withdrawal). A mild stretching sensation is considered normal.
results.female_result: Negative. The test is performed with the child seated or supine. The examiner abducts the arm, extends the elbow, and performs nerve-specific biasing maneuvers (like wrist extension and neck lateral flexion). Normal finding: No reproduction of radicular pain, paresthesia, or abnormal neurological symptoms. The child should not exhibit protective guarding or behavioral signs of distress (e.g., grimacing or withdrawal). A mild stretching sensation is considered normal.

results.male_result: Chest wall is flat and symmetric. Nipples are small, flat, and intact. No palpable breast tissue, masses, or nodules. No axillary lymphadenopathy.
results.female_result: Breasts are undeveloped (Tanner Stage 1). Chest wall is flat without palpable glandular tissue or breast buds. Nipples are small, flat, and symmetric without discharge. No masses, nodules, or axillary lymphadenopathy.

results.male_result: Negative: Less than 10 petechiae per 5 cm diameter circle (approximately 2.5 cm radius) on the volar surface of the forearm after 5 minutes of venous occlusion at a pressure midway between systolic and diastolic blood pressure. Skin appears normal without ecchymoses or purpura.
results.female_result: Negative: Less than 10 petechiae per 5 cm diameter circle (approximately 2.5 cm radius) on the volar surface of the forearm after 5 minutes of venous occlusion at a pressure midway between systolic and diastolic blood pressure. Skin appears normal without ecchymoses or purpura.

results.male_result: Typically 3-7 cm depending on age and height; absolute values increase as the child grows. (Values consistently < 2.5 cm are considered abnormal).
results.female_result: Typically 3-6 cm depending on age and height; absolute values increase as the child grows. (Values consistently < 2.5 cm are considered abnormal).

results.male_result: Visual Acuity: Age-dependent. Infants: Fixates and follows face/light; 3-5 years: 20/40 to 20/30 (6/12 to 6/9); 6+ years: 20/20 (6/6). Alignment: Orthophoric (eyes straight) in primary gaze; minimal intermittent deviation may be seen in neonates but should resolve by 3-4 months. Extraocular Movements: Full motility without nystagmus. Red Reflex: Bright, equal, and symmetrical bilaterally (Bruckner test). Pupils: Equal, round, reactive to light and accommodation. Intraocular Pressure: Generally lower than adults in infants (avg 10-12 mmHg), reaching adult levels by adolescence. Anterior Segment: Cornea clear (diameter >10mm in newborns); lens clear. Refraction: Physiological hyperopia (farsightedness) is normal in early childhood (+1.00 to +3.00 D). Fundoscopy: Optic nerve pink with distinct margins; macula and vessels normal.
results.female_result: Visual Acuity: Age-dependent. Infants: Fixates and follows face/light; 3-5 years: 20/40 to 20/30 (6/12 to 6/9); 6+ years: 20/20 (6/6). Alignment: Orthophoric (eyes straight) in primary gaze; minimal intermittent deviation may be seen in neonates but should resolve by 3-4 months. Extraocular Movements: Full motility without nystagmus. Red Reflex: Bright, equal, and symmetrical bilaterally (Bruckner test). Pupils: Equal, round, reactive to light and accommodation. Intraocular Pressure: Generally lower than adults in infants (avg 10-12 mmHg), reaching adult levels by adolescence. Anterior Segment: Cornea clear (diameter >10mm in newborns); lens clear. Refraction: Physiological hyperopia (farsightedness) is normal in early childhood (+1.00 to +3.00 D). Fundoscopy: Optic nerve pink with distinct margins; macula and vessels normal.

results.male_result: Cornea is clear, transparent, and lustrous with a smooth surface. No opacities, edema, ulcerations, or vascularization present. Corneal diameter is within normal limits for age. Corneal reflex is intact and brisk. No congenital anomalies (such as dermoids) or foreign bodies observed. Tear film is evenly distributed.
results.female_result: Cornea is clear, transparent, and lustrous with a smooth surface. No opacities, edema, ulcerations, or vascularization present. Corneal diameter is within normal limits for age. Corneal reflex is intact and brisk. No congenital anomalies (such as dermoids) or foreign bodies observed. Tear film is evenly distributed.

results.male_result: No movement of either eye observed during cover-uncover test; eyes maintain steady fixation with orthophoria (no latent or manifest strabismus); no refixation movement when cover is removed; binocular alignment is maintained in all cardinal positions of gaze.
results.female_result: No movement of either eye observed during cover-uncover test; eyes maintain steady fixation with orthophoria (no latent or manifest strabismus); no refixation movement when cover is removed; binocular alignment is maintained in all cardinal positions of gaze.

results.male_result: Normal skin architecture. Acquired melanocytic nevi predominately exhibit a globular pattern (uniformly distributed brown dots and globules) or a homogeneous structure; a reticular pigment network may be present but is less common than in adults. Peripheral globules arranged symmetrically are frequently observed, indicating normal physiological growth of the nevus. Absence of asymmetry, atypical vascular patterns, irregular streaks, or regression structures. No ulceration or bleeding.
results.female_result: Normal skin architecture. Acquired melanocytic nevi predominately exhibit a globular pattern (uniformly distributed brown dots and globules) or a homogeneous structure; a reticular pigment network may be present but is less common than in adults. Peripheral globules arranged symmetrically are frequently observed, indicating normal physiological growth of the nevus. Absence of asymmetry, atypical vascular patterns, irregular streaks, or regression structures. No ulceration or bleeding.

results.male_result: Anal sphincter tone is normal. Rectal mucosa is smooth without palpable masses, polyps (juvenile polyps), or tenderness. The prostate gland is rudimentary, undeveloped, and often not palpable; if felt, it is a small, flat structure, distinct from the developed gland in adults. No blood or abnormal discharge on examining finger upon withdrawal.
results.female_result: Anal sphincter tone is normal. Rectal mucosa is smooth without palpable masses, polyps, or tenderness. The uterus and cervix are small, undeveloped, and usually not distinctly palpable via rectum. No blood or abnormal discharge on examining finger upon withdrawal.

results.male_result: Negative test indicated by a firm end-feel. Although children may exhibit increased baseline physiological laxity (hypermobility) compared to adults, there should be no excessive medial joint line opening or medial clear space widening significantly greater than the uninjured side; no pain, pathological laxity, or clunking sensation observed.
results.female_result: Negative test indicated by a firm end-feel. Although children may exhibit increased baseline physiological laxity (hypermobility) compared to adults, there should be no excessive medial joint line opening or medial clear space widening significantly greater than the uninjured side; no pain, pathological laxity, or clunking sensation observed.

results.male_result: Values are age-dependent and generally lower than adults, typically ranging from 10 mm to 17 mm for children under 10 years old, increasing towards adult values (14-20 mm) in adolescence. Difference between both eyes ≤2 mm. Eyes are symmetrically positioned within the orbits.
results.female_result: Values are age-dependent and generally lower than adults, typically ranging from 10 mm to 17 mm for children under 10 years old, increasing towards adult values (14-20 mm) in adolescence. Difference between both eyes ≤2 mm. Eyes are symmetrically positioned within the orbits.

results.male_result: Eyelids: No ptosis, edema, or lesions; epicanthal folds may be present (common normal variant causing pseudostrabismus). Conjunctiva: Clear, no injection or discharge; pink and moist. Sclera: White; a slight bluish tint may be visible in younger children due to scleral thinness. Cornea: Clear, transparent, smooth light reflex. Red reflex: Present, bright, and symmetrical in both eyes (screening for retinoblastoma/cataracts). Pupils: Equal, round, reactive to light and accommodation (PERRLA); resting pupil size is often larger than in adults. Extraocular movements: Full range of motion; corneal light reflexes (Hirschberg test) are symmetrical and centered, indicating no strabismus.
results.female_result: Eyelids: No ptosis, edema, or lesions; epicanthal folds may be present (common normal variant causing pseudostrabismus). Conjunctiva: Clear, no injection or discharge; pink and moist. Sclera: White; a slight bluish tint may be visible in younger children due to scleral thinness. Cornea: Clear, transparent, smooth light reflex. Red reflex: Present, bright, and symmetrical in both eyes (screening for retinoblastoma/cataracts). Pupils: Equal, round, reactive to light and accommodation (PERRLA); resting pupil size is often larger than in adults. Extraocular movements: Full range of motion; corneal light reflexes (Hirschberg test) are symmetrical and centered, indicating no strabismus.

results.male_result: Penis: Pre-pubertal size and shape. Foreskin: May be non-retractable (physiological phimosis is common and normal in young children); no erythema, swelling, or purulent discharge. Urethral meatus: Centrally located at the tip of the glans (no hypospadias or epispadias). Scrotum: Symmetrical, rugated, no edema, discoloration, or acute swelling. Testes: Both descended and palpable within the scrotum, pre-pubertal size (volume < 4 ml, length usually < 2.5 cm), smooth surface, non-tender. Pubic Hair: Absent (Tanner Stage I) in pre-pubertal children. Inguinal region: No visible or palpable bulge suggestive of hernia; no lymphadenopathy.
results.female_result: Vulva: Pre-pubertal appearance; mucosa may appear thin and pale (childhood) or erythematous (infancy due to maternal estrogen withdrawal). Labia majora: Flat and less prominent than adults. Labia minora: Thin and non-prominent. Clitoris: Small, no hypertrophy. Urethral meatus: Clearly visible, no prolapse. Vaginal introitus: Hymen intact (annular or crescentic), no discharge, bleeding, foreign bodies, or labial adhesions. Pubic Hair: Absent (Tanner Stage I) in pre-pubertal children. Inguinal region: No hernias or lymphadenopathy.

results.male_result: Eyelids are symmetrical bilaterally with normal position and contour. No congenital ptosis, ectropion, or pathological entropion present. Epicanthal folds are frequently observed, particularly in younger children (a normal developmental variant). Eyelid margins are smooth and well-apposed to the globe. No edema, erythema, or swelling observed. Eyelashes are present and generally directed outward. No lesions, masses, or nodules palpable (e.g., dermoid cysts). Palpebral fissures are equal in width bilaterally; vertical height is age-dependent (approximately 6-10 mm, increasing with age). Lid closure is complete and symmetric. No lagophthalmos present.
results.female_result: Eyelids are symmetrical bilaterally with normal position and contour. No congenital ptosis, ectropion, or pathological entropion present. Epicanthal folds are frequently observed, particularly in younger children (a normal developmental variant). Eyelid margins are smooth and well-apposed to the globe. No edema, erythema, or swelling observed. Eyelashes are present and generally directed outward. No lesions, masses, or nodules palpable (e.g., dermoid cysts). Palpebral fissures are equal in width bilaterally; vertical height is age-dependent (approximately 6-10 mm, increasing with age). Lid closure is complete and symmetric. No lagophthalmos present.

results.male_result: Negative. Patient lies prone with knee flexed to 90 degrees. Examiner extends the hip by lifting the thigh off the examination table. No pain or discomfort is elicited in the anterior thigh or groin region. No radiation of pain along the L2-L4 dermatome distribution. The test does not reproduce any neurological symptoms.
results.female_result: Negative. Patient lies prone with knee flexed to 90 degrees. Examiner extends the hip by lifting the thigh off the examination table. No pain or discomfort is elicited in the anterior thigh or groin region. No radiation of pain along the L2-L4 dermatome distribution. The test does not reproduce any neurological symptoms.

results.male_result: Not applicable. This scoring system is designed to evaluate hirsutism in females.
results.female_result:
Pre-pubertal girls: Score 0 (Normal). 
Adolescent girls: Score < 8 (Normal).

results.male_result: Not applicable
results.female_result: Not applicable (examination only performed during pregnancy; normal fetal heart rate in pregnant women: 110-160 beats per minute, regular rhythm, best heard at the fetal back position)

results.male_result: 0-10 cm; Pre-pubescent boys typically exhibit high flexibility and can often touch the floor (0 cm). During the adolescent growth spurt (approx. ages 10-14), relative flexibility decreases significantly as bone growth outpaces muscle lengthening, potentially temporarily increasing the distance to 10-15 cm.
results.female_result: 0-5 cm; Pre-pubescent girls generally possess greater flexibility than boys and can usually touch the floor (0 cm) or place palms on the floor. While flexibility may slightly decrease during growth spurts, girls typically maintain the ability to reach within 5 cm of the floor throughout childhood.

results.male_result: Open angle configuration. In infants and young children, the angle is typically wide open. The trabecular meshwork is characteristically non-pigmented or very faintly pigmented and appears more translucent/glistening compared to adults. In infants, the iris insertion is often flat and may appear slightly more anterior, moving posteriorly with growth to reveal the ciliary body band. No peripheral anterior synechiae (PAS), no abnormal neovascularization, and no persistent mesodermal tissue (Barkan's membrane).
results.female_result: Open angle configuration. In infants and young children, the angle is typically wide open. The trabecular meshwork is characteristically non-pigmented or very faintly pigmented and appears more translucent/glistening compared to adults. In infants, the iris insertion is often flat and may appear slightly more anterior, moving posteriorly with growth to reveal the ciliary body band. No peripheral anterior synechiae (PAS), no abnormal neovascularization, and no persistent mesodermal tissue (Barkan's membrane).

results.male_result: In infants, normal hip abduction is significantly greater than in adults, typically ranging from 70 to 90 degrees when tested with the hips flexed. Abduction of less than 60 degrees may be considered restricted. In toddlers and school-aged children, the range generally settles between 50 and 60 degrees. The movement should be smooth, symmetrical, and painless, with no palpable 'clunk' or instability.
results.female_result: In infants, normal hip abduction typically ranges from 70 to 90 degrees with hips flexed. Since females are at higher risk for Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH), restricted abduction (often <60 degrees) or asymmetry is a critical warning sign. In older female children, flexibility remains high, typically 50-60 degrees. Movement must be smooth and symmetrical without pain or clicking sounds.

results.male_result: Nasopharyngeal mucosa is pink and moist. Physiological adenoid tissue (pharyngeal tonsil) is typically present and visible on the superior and posterior walls; size varies with age (peaking around ages 3-7) but should not completely obstruct the posterior nares (choanae). Eustachian tube orifices are patent, though the angle is more horizontal than in adults. No purulent secretions, pathological erythema, or ulcerative lesions observed.
results.female_result: Nasopharyngeal mucosa is pink and moist. Physiological adenoid tissue (pharyngeal tonsil) is typically present and visible on the superior and posterior walls; size varies with age (peaking around ages 3-7) but should not completely obstruct the posterior nares (choanae). Eustachian tube orifices are patent, though the angle is more horizontal than in adults. No purulent secretions, pathological erythema, or ulcerative lesions observed.

results.male_result: Mental status: Alert, responsive, and meeting age-appropriate developmental milestones (language, social interaction, motor skills); oriented to surroundings (if age-appropriate). Cranial nerves: Intact bilaterally (assessed via observation of tracking, sucking, and facial symmetry in infants; standard testing in older children); pupils PERRLA. Motor: Muscle tone and bulk normal for age; spontaneous movements symmetric; strength 5/5 or appropriate functional strength against gravity/resistance. Sensory: Responds symmetrically to tactile and painful stimuli. Reflexes: Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric; primitive reflexes (e.g., Moro, palmar grasp) absent in children >6 months; plantar response flexor (Babinski negative) in older children, though extensor response is normal in infants <12-18 months. Coordination: Reaches for objects accurately; gait pattern normal for developmental stage (wide-based in toddlers, narrow in older children).
results.female_result: Mental status: Alert, responsive, and meeting age-appropriate developmental milestones (language, social interaction, motor skills); oriented to surroundings (if age-appropriate). Cranial nerves: Intact bilaterally (assessed via observation of tracking, sucking, and facial symmetry in infants; standard testing in older children); pupils PERRLA. Motor: Muscle tone and bulk normal for age; spontaneous movements symmetric; strength 5/5 or appropriate functional strength against gravity/resistance. Sensory: Responds symmetrically to tactile and painful stimuli. Reflexes: Deep tendon reflexes 2+ and symmetric; primitive reflexes (e.g., Moro, palmar grasp) absent in children >6 months; plantar response flexor (Babinski negative) in older children, though extensor response is normal in infants <12-18 months. Coordination: Reaches for objects accurately; gait pattern normal for developmental stage (wide-based in toddlers, narrow in older children).

results.male_result: 0 cm (occiput touches the wall with ease while standing with heels and back against the wall, chin held level, eyes looking straight ahead; no gap between the occiput and wall surface)
results.female_result: 0 cm (occiput touches the wall with ease while standing with heels and back against the wall, chin held level, eyes looking straight ahead; no gap between the occiput and wall surface)

results.male_result: Full extraocular movements in all six cardinal directions of gaze. Conjugate eye movements are present without limitation or lag. No nystagmus observed. No strabismus (heterotropia) or significant latent deviation detected; corneal light reflexes are central and symmetrical (Hirschberg test). Smooth pursuit and saccadic movements are intact. Convergence is robust, typically with a near point of convergence of 6 cm or less.
results.female_result: Full extraocular movements in all six cardinal directions of gaze. Conjugate eye movements are present without limitation or lag. No nystagmus observed. No strabismus (heterotropia) or significant latent deviation detected; corneal light reflexes are central and symmetrical (Hirschberg test). Smooth pursuit and saccadic movements are intact. Convergence is robust, typically with a near point of convergence of 6 cm or less.

results.male_result: Red reflex is present, bright, and symmetric bilaterally. Optic disc appears pink to reddish-orange with sharp, well-defined margins; in very young infants, the disc may appear slightly paler than in adults. Cup-to-disc ratio is typically small (<0.3). Retinal vessels show a normal arteriovenous ratio of 2:3, with straight or slightly curved courses. The macula appears darker than the surrounding retina, and the foveal light reflex is typically very distinct, bright, and glistening (more prominent than in adults). The retinal background is uniform without hemorrhages, exudates, or abnormal pigmentation.
results.female_result: Red reflex is present, bright, and symmetric bilaterally. Optic disc appears pink to reddish-orange with sharp, well-defined margins; in very young infants, the disc may appear slightly paler than in adults. Cup-to-disc ratio is typically small (<0.3). Retinal vessels show a normal arteriovenous ratio of 2:3, with straight or slightly curved courses. The macula appears darker than the surrounding retina, and the foveal light reflex is typically very distinct, bright, and glistening (more prominent than in adults). The retinal background is uniform without hemorrhages, exudates, or abnormal pigmentation.

results.male_result: Lips: Pink, moist, no cyanosis or fissures. Oral mucosa: Pink, moist, intact, no thrush (candidiasis), no Koplik spots. Gingiva: Pink, firm; may be slightly swollen over erupting teeth, otherwise no bleeding or inflammation. Teeth: Deciduous (primary) or mixed dentition appropriate for age; total 20 primary teeth typically present by age 2.5-3; mixed dentition typically begins around age 6. No visible caries (specifically nursing bottle caries). Tongue: Pink, moist, midline protrusion, no ankyloglossia (tongue-tie). Hard/Soft Palate: Intact, no clefts. Uvula: Midline, elevates symmetrically. Tonsils: May appear relatively larger due to physiologic lymphoid hypertrophy (Grade 1-3) but without erythema or exudates. Pharynx: Pink, no inflammation or cobbling.
results.female_result: Lips: Pink, moist, no cyanosis or fissures. Oral mucosa: Pink, moist, intact, no thrush (candidiasis), no Koplik spots. Gingiva: Pink, firm; may be slightly swollen over erupting teeth, otherwise no bleeding or inflammation. Teeth: Deciduous (primary) or mixed dentition appropriate for age; total 20 primary teeth typically present by age 2.5-3; mixed dentition typically begins around age 6. No visible caries (specifically nursing bottle caries). Tongue: Pink, moist, midline protrusion, no ankyloglossia (tongue-tie). Hard/Soft Palate: Intact, no clefts. Uvula: Midline, elevates symmetrically. Tonsils: May appear relatively larger due to physiologic lymphoid hypertrophy (Grade 1-3) but without erythema or exudates. Pharynx: Pink, no inflammation or cobbling.

results.male_result: Oral mucosa is pink, moist, and intact without lesions (e.g., Koplik spots, thrush). Dentition is appropriate for age (primary/deciduous or mixed dentition), with no evidence of nursing bottle caries or gross decay. Tongue is midline, pink, moist, with normal mobility; frenulum is of normal length (no ankyloglossia). Hard and soft palate are intact with no evidence of clefts. Uvula is midline and elevates symmetrically. Tonsils are often physiologically larger than in adults (Grade 1-2 is considered normal due to lymphoid tissue hyperplasia), symmetric, pink, and without exudate, deep pitting, or acute erythema. Posterior pharyngeal wall is pink without exudate or cobblestoning. Gag reflex is intact.
results.female_result: Oral mucosa is pink, moist, and intact without lesions (e.g., Koplik spots, thrush). Dentition is appropriate for age (primary/deciduous or mixed dentition), with no evidence of nursing bottle caries or gross decay. Tongue is midline, pink, moist, with normal mobility; frenulum is of normal length (no ankyloglossia). Hard and soft palate are intact with no evidence of clefts. Uvula is midline and elevates symmetrically. Tonsils are often physiologically larger than in adults (Grade 1-2 is considered normal due to lymphoid tissue hyperplasia), symmetric, pink, and without exudate, deep pitting, or acute erythema. Posterior pharyngeal wall is pink without exudate or cobblestoning. Gag reflex is intact.

results.male_result: Supine: Blood pressure and heart rate must be within normal age-specific and height-specific percentiles (e.g., School-age 6-12y: HR 75-118 bpm, Systolic BP 97-115 mmHg). Standing (after 1-3 minutes): Systolic blood pressure decrease <20 mmHg, Diastolic blood pressure decrease <10 mmHg. Heart rate increase <40 bpm. No symptoms of dizziness, lightheadedness, pallor, or syncope upon position change.
results.female_result: Supine: Blood pressure and heart rate must be within normal age-specific and height-specific percentiles (e.g., School-age 6-12y: HR 75-118 bpm, Systolic BP 97-115 mmHg). Standing (after 1-3 minutes): Systolic blood pressure decrease <20 mmHg, Diastolic blood pressure decrease <10 mmHg. Heart rate increase <40 bpm. No symptoms of dizziness, lightheadedness, pallor, or syncope upon position change.

results.male_result: External auditory canal is patent and free of foreign bodies. The tympanic membrane is intact, pearly gray, translucent, and demonstrates normal mobility on pneumatic otoscopy. The cone of light reflex is visible at the 5 o'clock position (right ear) and 7 o'clock position (left ear). Bony landmarks (malleus handle and umbo) are clearly visible. No perforation, retraction, bulging, or middle ear effusion (fluid) is present. No signs of infection or cholesteatoma.
results.female_result: External auditory canal is patent and free of foreign bodies. The tympanic membrane is intact, pearly gray, translucent, and demonstrates normal mobility on pneumatic otoscopy. The cone of light reflex is visible at the 5 o'clock position (right ear) and 7 o'clock position (left ear). Bony landmarks (malleus handle and umbo) are clearly visible. No perforation, retraction, bulging, or middle ear effusion (fluid) is present. No signs of infection or cholesteatoma.

results.male_result: Negative. With the patient supine and knee extended, the examiner applies downward pressure on the superior pole of the patella while the patient contracts the quadriceps muscle. Normal finding: No pain or crepitus is elicited. The patella glides smoothly under compression without discomfort. The patient can maintain quadriceps contraction against resistance without pain.
results.female_result: Negative. With the patient supine and knee extended, the examiner applies downward pressure on the superior pole of the patella while the patient contracts the quadriceps muscle. Normal finding: No pain or crepitus is elicited. The patella glides smoothly under compression without discomfort. The patient can maintain quadriceps contraction against resistance without pain.

results.male_result: Negative. With the child supine and knee extended, compression of the suprapatellar pouch followed by a sharp downward tap on the patella produces no palpable click or floating sensation. No ballottement is detected, indicating the absence of significant knee joint effusion.
results.female_result: Negative. With the child supine and knee extended, compression of the suprapatellar pouch followed by a sharp downward tap on the patella produces no palpable click or floating sensation. No ballottement is detected, indicating the absence of significant knee joint effusion.

results.male_result: Negative. The patient lies supine. In younger children, the examiner passively positions the leg; older children may perform the movement actively. The tested hip is flexed, abducted, and externally rotated, placing the lateral malleolus on the contralateral knee (Figure-4 position). Gentle downward pressure allows the thigh to fall freely toward the examination table. Due to greater physiological flexibility and ligamentous laxity in children, the knee often reaches the table level easily without restriction. No pain (verbal complaint or behavioral guarding) is elicited in the hip, sacroiliac joint, or groin region.

results.male_result: Negative. No pain or discomfort elicited upon bilateral compression of the iliac crests toward midline (compression test) or upon outward pressure on the anterior superior iliac spines (distraction test). The pediatric pelvis is stable with no crepitus, no abnormal movement, and no tenderness over the sacroiliac joints or pubic symphysis.

results.male_result: Not applicable (Pelvic examination is a gynecological exam). For pediatric males, a Genitourinary Examination is performed: Testes descended bilaterally into the scrotum, no inguinal hernia or hydrocele. Urethral meatus positioned at the tip of the glans. Foreskin may be non-retractable (physiological phimosis) in infants and young children.
results.female_result: Examination is typically limited to external visual inspection only (often in the frog-leg position). External genitalia: Pre-pubertal appearance (Tanner Stage 1, no pubic hair). Labia majora are flat; labia minora are thin and unestrogenized. Hymen is intact (commonly annular or crescentic) with smooth edges. Vaginal introitus mucosa appears thin and redder compared to adults due to low estrogen levels. No discharge, bleeding, lesions, masses, or labial adhesions (synechiae). Internal speculum and bimanual examinations are NOT performed routinely.

results.male_result: Penile dimensions vary significantly according to age and developmental stage. For full-term newborns, the mean stretched penile length is approximately 3.5 cm (±0.4 cm). During early to mid-childhood (pre-puberty), growth is gradual, with mean stretched lengths typically ranging from 4 cm to 6 cm. The penis should appear normal in structure; the urethral meatus should be centrally located at the tip of the glans (no hypospadias). In uncircumcised young children, the foreskin is often tight and non-retractable (physiologic phimosis), which is a normal finding.
results.female_result: Not applicable

results.male_result: Probing depth: Generally 1-2 mm, though up to 3 mm is acceptable especially around erupting teeth (pseudo-pockets); No bleeding on probing (BOP); No suppuration; Gingiva typically appears more reddish, smoother (less stippled), and flaccid with rounded or rolled margins compared to adults; No clinical attachment loss.
results.female_result: Probing depth: Generally 1-2 mm, though up to 3 mm is acceptable especially around erupting teeth (pseudo-pockets); No bleeding on probing (BOP); No suppuration; Gingiva typically appears more reddish, smoother (less stippled), and flaccid with rounded or rolled margins compared to adults; No clinical attachment loss.

results.male_result: Negative - No paresthesia, numbness, or tingling in the median nerve distribution after maintaining maximal wrist flexion. results.male_result: General appearance: Alert, cooperative (age-appropriate), well-nourished, no acute distress. Vital signs: Age-specific ranges apply. Heart rate: Newborn 100-160 bpm, Infant 90-150 bpm, Toddler 80-140 bpm, School-age 70-110 bpm, Adolescent 60-100 bpm. Respiratory rate: Newborn 30-60 breaths/min, Infant 24-40 breaths/min, School-age 18-30 breaths/min, Adolescent 12-20 breaths/min. Blood pressure: Normotensive for age, sex, and height (typically <90th percentile). Temperature: 36.4-37.5°C (97.5-99.5°F). Growth: Height, weight, and BMI-for-age between the 5th and 85th percentiles on standardized growth charts. Head: Normocephalic, atraumatic; fontanelles closed (if >18 months). Eyes: Pupils equal, round, reactive to light (PERRLA), red reflex present bilaterally, extraocular movements intact. Ears: External canals clear, tympanic membranes intact and mobile. Nose: Nasal mucosa pink, no discharge. Throat: Oropharynx clear; tonsils may be 1+ or 2+ (normal physiologic hypertrophy in childhood). Neck: Supple, no significant lymphadenopathy (small 'shotty' cervical nodes may be present), thyroid non-enlarged. Chest: Symmetric expansion, clear to auscultation bilaterally. Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm, S1 and S2 normal, physiologic S3 or innocent murmurs may be present, peripheral pulses 2+. Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, liver edge may be palpable 1-2 cm below the costal margin in young children; no masses. Musculoskeletal: Full range of motion, no scoliosis, normal gait. Neurological: Developmental milestones appropriate for age, cranial nerves II-XII intact, motor strength 5/5, deep tendon reflexes 2+. Genitourinary: Testes descended bilaterally, no hernias, Tanner stage appropriate for age. Skin: Warm, dry, no suspicious rashes or lesions.

results.male_result: Negative - During forward flexion from standing position, both posterior superior iliac spines (PSIS) move symmetrically and superiorly. No asymmetric elevation or restricted movement of either PSIS is observed. The sacroiliac joints demonstrate normal, symmetric mobility.

results.male_result: External auditory canal is patent and free of obstruction. Tympanic membrane is intact, translucent, and pearly gray. Landmarks (malleus handle and umbo) are visible. In infants, the tympanic membrane may appear more horizontal and the cone of light may be diffuse; in older children, the cone of light is clearly visible in the anteroinferior quadrant. Upon insufflation, the tympanic membrane demonstrates normal mobility with brisk movement, indicating normal middle ear pressure and Eustachian tube function. No retraction, bulging, perforation, or effusion noted.
results.female_result: External auditory canal is patent and free of obstruction. Tympanic membrane is intact, translucent, and pearly gray. Landmarks (malleus handle and umbo) are visible. Upon insufflation, the tympanic membrane demonstrates normal mobility with brisk movement, indicating normal middle ear pressure and Eustachian tube function. No retraction, bulging, perforation, or effusion noted.

results.male_result: Abdomen is soft and non-tender; a protuberant abdomen ('pot-belly') is normal in toddlers due to weak abdominal muscles. No abnormal masses. The liver edge is frequently palpable 1-2 cm below the right costal margin in infants and young children, which is a normal physiological finding. The spleen is usually not palpable, though the tip may be felt in some healthy infants. Kidneys are generally not palpable (except potentially in neonates). Small, mobile, non-tender, 'shotty' superficial lymph nodes are commonly palpable in the cervical and inguinal regions. Thyroid gland is not enlarged. Peripheral pulses are palpable, strong, and symmetric. In infants, the anterior fontanelle is palpable, flat, and soft.
results.female_result: Abdomen is soft and non-tender; a protuberant abdomen ('pot-belly') is normal in toddlers due to weak abdominal muscles. No abnormal masses. The spleen is usually not palpable, though the tip may be felt in some healthy infants. Kidneys are generally not palpable (except potentially in neonates). Small, mobile, non-tender, 'shotty' superficial lymph nodes are commonly palpable in the cervical and inguinal regions. Thyroid gland is not enlarged. For school-age children and adolescents: Normal response involves maintaining balance with 1-2 quick corrective backward steps. The child should quickly recover the upright position without falling or requiring assistance. Reflexes should be symmetrical.

results.male_result: Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light and accommodation (PERRLA). Direct and consensual light reflexes are brisk. Pupil size in children is typically larger than in adults, often ranging from 3 to 7 mm depending on lighting. No afferent pupillary defect detected.

results.male_result: Bilateral red reflexes are present, symmetric, and bright in both eyes. The reflex should be identical in color, intensity, and clarity. When viewed simultaneously (Brückner test), the reflexes are equal and homogeneous, typically appearing as a reddish-orange or yellow-orange glow. No dark spots, white reflexes (leukocoria), or asymmetry in the size or brightness of the reflexes are observed.
results.female_result: Bilateral red reflexes are present, symmetric, and bright in both eyes. The reflex should be identical in color, intensity, and clarity. When viewed simultaneously (Brückner test), the reflexes are equal and homogeneous, typically appearing as a reddish-orange or yellow-orange glow. No dark spots, white reflexes (leukocoria), or asymmetry in the size or brightness of the reflexes are observed.

results.male_result: ≥5 cm increase in distance. (Note: Healthy children typically exhibit greater flexibility than adults, often expanding >6-7 cm. Values near the 5 cm threshold in a previously flexible child may require clinical correlation.)
results.female_result: ≥5 cm increase in distance. (Note: Healthy children typically exhibit greater flexibility than adults, often expanding >6-7 cm. Values near the 5 cm threshold in a previously flexible child may require clinical correlation.)

results.male_result: Not applicable for infants and young children (typically under 6-8 years) due to insufficient balance and motor coordination. For school-age children and adolescents: Able to maintain single-leg balance with controlled descent. While the biomechanical ideal is identical to adults (knee tracking over toe, level pelvis), slight postural sway or reduced squat depth is common and may be considered normal variation due to ongoing neuromuscular development and growth spurts.
results.female_result: Not applicable for infants and young children (typically under 6-8 years). For school-age children and adolescents: Able to maintain single-leg balance with controlled descent. During puberty, females may demonstrate a slightly higher physiological tendency for dynamic knee valgus compared to males due to widening of the pelvis and Q-angle changes; however, excessive valgus collapse remains a risk factor and is not considered 'normal' biomechanics.

results.male_result: Skin is warm, dry, intact, and soft with excellent turgor. Color is appropriate for ethnicity without pathological pallor, cyanosis, or jaundice. Benign birthmarks (e.g., congenital dermal melanocytosis) may be present. No rashes, lesions, petechiae, or ecchymoses. Hair distribution is comprised of fine vellus hair; no terminal hair in axillary, pubic, or facial regions is present (in pre-pubertal children). Nails are smooth and pink with capillary refill less than 2 seconds. No edema or lymphadenopathy.

results.male_result: Skin: Smooth and soft texture, normal color consistent with ethnicity, warm and dry to touch. Good turgor and elasticity (immediate recoil). Presence of benign birthmarks (e.g., congenital dermal melanocytosis, nevus simplex) is considered within normal limits. Capillary refill time less than 2 seconds. Sclera: White and clear (a slight bluish tint may be observed in healthy infants), no icterus, injection, or hemorrhage. Conjunctivae pink and moist.

results.item_name: Slit Lamp Examination with Fluorescein Staining
results.male_result: Cornea: Clear and transparent with smooth epithelial surface, no fluorescein uptake indicating intact corneal epithelium, no punctate staining, dendritic lesions, or ulcerations. Tear film: Stable with tear break-up time (TBUT) ≥10 seconds (often higher in children, typically 15-20 seconds), uniform fluorescein distribution. Conjunctiva: No staining or minimal physiological staining at the limbus. Anterior chamber: Quiet, no cells or flare; depth is appropriate for age. Lens: Clear, no opacities. Eyelids: Normal lid margins with no meibomian gland dysfunction or inflammation.

results.male_result: Lateral flexion range of motion is typically greater than in adults due to natural physiological ligamentous laxity and intervertebral disc hydration. No pain, stiffness, or muscle spasm is observed.

results.male_result:
Pediatric values are significantly higher than adults due to rapid skeletal growth and modeling. Levels vary drastically by age and Tanner stage. 
General ranges (highly assay-dependent):
- P1NP: 60-1200 ng/mL (Highest in infants <1y and pubertal boys 12-15y).
- Osteocalcin: 25-140 ng/mL.
- BSAP: 40-160 μg/L.
- CTX: 0.4-2.8 ng/mL.
- NTX: 10-100 nM BCE/L.
Note: Values peak during the pubertal growth spurt (approx. age 12-15) and decline toward adult levels after epiphyseal closure (approx. age 17-19).
results.female_result:
Pediatric values are significantly higher than adults due to rapid skeletal growth and modeling. Levels vary drastically by age and Tanner stage. 
General ranges (highly assay-dependent):
- P1NP: 60-1200 ng/mL (Highest in infants <1y and pubertal girls 10-13y).
- Osteocalcin: 25-140 ng/mL.
- BSAP: 40-160 μg/L.
- CTX: 0.4-2.8 ng/mL.
- NTX: 10-100 nM BCE/L.
Note: The pubertal peak occurs earlier in females (approx. age 10-13) compared to males. Values decline rapidly after menarche and the cessation of linear growth (approx. age 15-16).

results.male_result: Red blood cells: Generally normocytic and normochromic relative to age (Note: Infants and young children have lower MCV and Hb limits compared to adults; neonates show macrocytosis and polychromasia); no significant poikilocytosis. White blood cells: Normal morphology. Differential count varies significantly by age: From approx. 2 weeks to 4-6 years of age, there is a physiological lymphocyte predominance (Lymphocytes 45-65%, Neutrophils 25-45%); older children shift toward the adult neutrophil-predominant pattern. Reactive lymphocytes are common. No blast cells. Platelets: Adequate number (150,000-450,000/μL), normal size and morphology.

results.male_result:
Reference ranges are significantly age-dependent, with values generally higher in neonates and infants compared to adults. 

1. Premature/Neonates (1-4 days): TSH surges immediately after birth (physiologic TSH surge) and can range from 1.0 to 39.0 mIU/L. 
2. Infants (2 weeks - 1 year): TSH: 1.7-9.1 mIU/L; Free T4: 0.8-2.2 ng/dL; Total T4: 6.0-16.0 μg/dL. 
3. Children (1-12 years): TSH: 0.7-6.4 mIU/L; Free T4: 0.8-2.0 ng/dL; Total T4: 6.0-15.0 μg/dL. 
4. Adolescents (13-18 years): Ranges gradually approach adult values (TSH: 0.5-4.3 mIU/L; Free T4: 0.8-1.9 ng/dL).

results.male_result: Normal stool color varies by age and diet. Infants (Breastfed): Typically golden yellow, mustard-colored, or soft yellow. Infants (Formula-fed): Typically pale yellow to yellow-brown or tan. Older Children: Yellow-brown to brown, similar to adults. On the standard infant stool color card (for biliary atresia screening), colors graded 4, 5, 6 (and 7) are considered normal pigmentation. No clay-colored, pale/white (grades 1-3), black tarry, or bloody stools.

results.male_result: HLA typing results are reported as specific allele designations for each locus. Normal finding: Successful identification of HLA alleles at Class I (HLA-A, HLA-B, HLA-C) and Class II (HLA-DR, HLA-DQ, HLA-DP) loci. Results expressed in standard nomenclature (e.g., HLA-A*02:01, HLA-B*07:02, HLA-DRB1*15:01). Each individual inherits one allele from each parent, resulting in heterozygous or homozygous expression at each locus. No 'normal' or 'abnormal' alleles exist; results represent individual genetic polymorphism.

results.male_result: Immunoglobulins: Highly age-dependent. IgG levels are high at birth (maternal transfer), drop to a nadir (approx. 2.0-7.0 g/L) between 3-6 months, and gradually rise to adult levels by adolescence. IgA and IgM are low at birth and increase gradually with age. IgE generally <100 IU/mL. Complement: C3 and C4 levels reach adult ranges by 6-12 months. Lymphocyte subsets: Infants and young children have higher absolute lymphocyte counts and percentage of B cells (CD19+ 15-40% in infants) and CD4+ T cells compared to adults. The CD4/CD8 ratio is typically higher in infancy (often >2.0). Autoantibodies: Negative (ANA <1:40, RF <20 IU/mL). CRP <5.0 mg/L. ESR: Generally 0-10 mm/hr for children, 0-15 mm/hr for adolescents.
results.female_result: Immunoglobulins: Highly age-dependent. IgG levels are high at birth, drop to a nadir (approx. 2.0-7.0 g/L) between 3-6 months, and gradually rise to adult levels by adolescence. IgA and IgM are low at birth and increase gradually. IgE generally <100 IU/mL. Complement: C3 and C4 levels reach adult ranges by 6-12 months. Lymphocyte subsets: Infants and young children have higher absolute lymphocyte counts, higher percentage of B cells (CD19+ 15-40%), and CD4+ T cells compared to adults. The CD4/CD8 ratio is typically higher in infancy. Autoantibodies: Negative (ANA <1:40, RF <20 IU/mL). CRP <5.0 mg/L. ESR: Generally 0-10 mm/hr for children, 0-20 mm/hr for adolescents.

results.male_result:
Reference ranges are highly age-dependent and absolute counts are significantly higher in infants and young children compared to adults.

Infants (0-12 months): Total Lymphocytes: 3,000-10,000 cells/μL; CD3+ T cells: 50-80% (2,000-6,000 cells/μL); CD4+ T helper cells: 35-60% (1,500-4,000 cells/μL); CD8+ T cytotoxic cells: 15-35% (800-1,800 cells/μL); CD19+ B cells: 15-35% (600-3,000 cells/μL, significantly higher than adults); CD16+CD56+ NK cells: 5-25%.

Children (1-10 years): Total Lymphocytes: 2,000-8,000 cells/μL; CD4+ T cells: 30-50% (700-2,500 cells/μL); CD19+ B cells: 15-30%.

Adolescents (11-18 years): Values gradually approach adult ranges (Total Lymphocytes: 1,500-4,500 cells/μL).
results.female_result:
Reference ranges are highly age-dependent and absolute counts are significantly higher in infants and young children compared to adults.

Infants (0-12 months): Total Lymphocytes: 3,000-10,000 cells/μL; CD3+ T cells: 50-80% (2,000-6,000 cells/μL); CD4+ T helper cells: 35-60% (1,500-4,000 cells/μL); CD8+ T cytotoxic cells: 15-35% (800-1,800 cells/μL); CD19+ B cells: 15-35% (600-3,000 cells/μL, significantly higher than adults); CD16+CD56+ NK cells: 5-25%.

Adolescents (11-18 years): Values gradually approach adult ranges (Total Lymphocytes: 1,500-4,500 cells/μL).

results.male_result: Normal immunoelectrophoresis pattern showing age-appropriate immunoglobulin distribution. Quantitative levels of IgG, IgA, and IgM differ significantly from adults and must be interpreted based on specific age-matched reference ranges. Typically, IgG levels are high at birth (maternal transfer), drop to a nadir around 3-6 months (physiological hypogammaglobulinemia), and gradually rise to adult levels. IgA and IgM levels are low at birth and increase gradually with age. Normal polyclonal immunoglobulin distribution with symmetric arcs. Kappa and lambda light chains present in normal ratio. No monoclonal bands (M-protein) detected.

Unlike adults, Lactobacillus species are scant or absent. The environment is neutral to alkaline (pH 6.5-7.5). Negative for specific pediatric pathogens such as Group A Streptococcus (Streptococcus pyogenes), Haemophilus influenzae, and Shigella species, as well as sexually transmitted organisms.

results.male_result: Non-specific bowel gas distribution is observed with no evidence of distinct bowel dilation, obstruction, or pneumatosis intestinalis. No free intraperitoneal air is visualized. Psoas muscle margins may be indistinct, which can be normal in younger children due to a relative lack of retroperitoneal fat. No radio-opaque foreign bodies or abnormal calcifications are identified.

results.male_result: The colon demonstrates normal caliber, length, and rotation appropriate for the child's age and size. The haustral pattern is normal for the developmental stage. The ileocecal valve is intact, and reflux into the terminal ileum is frequently visualized and normal.

results.male_result: Interproximal surfaces of primary and/or permanent posterior teeth show intact enamel and dentin with no evidence of radiolucent areas indicating caries. Developing permanent tooth buds may be visible apical to or between the roots of primary teeth. Alveolar bone crest is located 1-2 mm apical to the cementoenamel junction (CEJ). No calculus or periapical pathology detected.

results.male_result: Lungs: Bilateral lung fields are clear with no focal consolidation or air bronchograms. In infants and young children, the thymus is frequently visible in the superior mediastinum (appearing as a 'sail sign' or 'wave sign'), which is a normal physiological finding. Heart: Cardiac silhouette is normal in configuration. The cardiothoracic ratio (CTR) is variable by age; up to 0.60 (60%) is normal in neonates and infants, decreasing to < 0.50 in older children. Mediastinum: Trachea is midline. Hila: Bilateral hilar structures are normal. Pleura: No pleural effusion or thickening. Diaphragm: Smooth contours. Bony thorax: Osseous structures show normal development for age with open growth plates (physes). Ribs are more horizontally oriented in infants. Soft tissues: Unremarkable.

Mediastinum: Trachea is midline. Hila: Bilateral hilar structures are normal. Pleura: No pleural effusion or thickening. Diaphragm: Smooth contours. Soft tissues: Unremarkable; breast tissue is typically not prominent in prepubertal girls.

results.male_result: Bladder position varies with age; in infants and young children, the bladder is largely an abdominal organ, descending into the pelvis with growth. The bladder contour is smooth and regular. No filling defects, diverticula, or urachal anomalies observed. Bladder wall thickness is uniform. No vesicoureteral reflux (VUR) demonstrated during filling or voiding phases. Bladder capacity is age-appropriate (significantly smaller than adults, calculated based on age). During voiding, the urethra is patent; specifically, there is no evidence of posterior urethral valves (PUV), strictures, or obstruction. Post-void residual is minimal.
results.female_result: Bladder position varies with age; in infants and young children, the bladder is largely an abdominal organ, descending into the pelvis with growth. The contour is smooth and regular without filling defects or diverticula. Bladder wall thickness is uniform. No vesicoureteral reflux (VUR) demonstrated during filling or voiding phases. Bladder capacity is age-appropriate (significantly smaller than adults, calculated based on age). During voiding, the urethra appears normal in caliber and course without obstruction. No evidence of ureteroceles or ectopic ureters is observed. Post-void residual is minimal.

results.female_result: Patent lacrimal drainage system with contrast medium flowing freely from the puncta through the canaliculi into the lacrimal sac, then through the nasolacrimal duct into the inferior meatus of the nasal cavity. The lacrimal sac and nasolacrimal duct demonstrate normal size and caliber proportionate to the child's age and craniofacial development. No filling defects, strictures, or obstructions observed.

results.male_result: At rest: Anorectal angle (ARA) is within normal physiological limits (generally 70-110 degrees). During straining/defecation: Anorectal angle widens (increases), puborectalis muscle relaxes completely, and pelvic floor descends appropriately for body size (absolute descent distance is less than the adult 1-3 cm range). Complete evacuation of contrast material occurs efficiently. No evidence of paradoxical puborectalis contraction (anismus). Anal canal length is shorter than adult values and proportionate to age. No megarectum or intussusception.
results.female_result: At rest: Anorectal angle (ARA) is within normal physiological limits. During straining/defecation: Anorectal angle widens, puborectalis muscle relaxes completely. Complete evacuation of contrast material occurs efficiently. Unlike adult women, anterior rectoceles, cystoceles, or enteroceles are not observed in normal pediatric females. No evidence of paradoxical puborectalis contraction. Anal canal length is proportionate to age.

results.male_result: Dentition is consistent with the child's chronological age (primary, mixed, or early permanent dentition). In primary dentition, 20 deciduous teeth are present. In mixed dentition, a combination of deciduous and permanent teeth is observed. Physiological root resorption of primary teeth is present where permanent successors are erupting. Developing permanent tooth germs (buds) are visible within the alveolar bone in correct anatomical positions. Newly erupted permanent teeth may show incomplete root formation with open apices (wide apical foramen) and relatively large pulp chambers. Periodontal ligament space is uniform. Lamina dura is intact, though naturally interrupted in areas of active eruption. Alveolar bone shows normal trabecular pattern. No evidence of caries, pathological periapical radiolucency, cysts, or congenital anomalies (e.g., supernumerary teeth or congenital absence).

results.male_result: Both kidneys demonstrate normal size, shape, and position appropriate for the child's age and height (renal length correlates with somatic growth). Persistent fetal lobulation (wavy contour) may be evident in infants and young children, which is a normal variant. Parenchymal thickness is normal. Prompt and symmetric nephrogram phase is observed. Calyces are cup-shaped with sharp fornices. Renal pelvis and ureters show normal caliber relative to body size, with smooth courses and physiologic peristalsis. The urinary bladder is normal in capacity and shape for age; in infants and young children, the bladder is largely an abdominal organ, positioning lower into the pelvis as the child grows. Post-void film shows adequate emptying.

results.male_result: Both kidneys are visualized with size, shape, and position appropriate for age and height. Renal outlines are smooth. results.male_result: Normal lumbar lordosis is maintained (or shows age-appropriate curvature). Five lumbar vertebrae are present with normal alignment; no evidence of scoliosis or listhesis. Pedicles are intact. No evidence of spondylolysis. Paraspinal soft tissues are unremarkable.

results.male_result: Mastoid development and pneumatization are age-dependent. In infants, the mastoid process is small, and air cells are usually not fully developed (only the antrum may be visible). In young children, pneumatization is progressive. By older childhood and adolescence, appearances resemble adults with well-pneumatized bilateral mastoid air cells and normal trabecular patterns. No evidence of pathological opacification, bony destruction, or soft tissue swelling. External auditory canals are patent.

results.male_result: Nasal bones show ossification appropriate for age, appearing smaller and less dense than in adults. Cortical margins are smooth. The nasal septum is midline. Paranasal sinus aeration varies by age (frontal sinuses may be absent or undeveloped in young children). No soft tissue swelling is observed.

Retropharyngeal soft tissue thickness is normal (less than the anterior-posterior diameter of the C2 vertebral body). Cervical spine alignment is normal.

results.male_result: Cervical spine alignment is appropriate for age. Physiological pseudosubluxation of C2 on C3 (and occasionally C3 on C4) may be present due to ligamentous laxity (common in children <8 years). Normal anterior wedging of vertebral bodies C3 to C5 is noted. Visible synchondroses (growth plates), particularly the subdental synchondrosis (in children <7 years) and the neurocentral synchondroses, are present and distinct from fractures. The atlas-dens interval (ADI) is within normal limits (up to 4-5 mm is normal in young children). Prevertebral soft tissue thickness is normal for the respiratory phase. No osseous abnormalities or congenital defects.

results.male_result: Dental development is consistent with the chronological age. Depending on the specific age, either a full primary dentition (20 teeth) or mixed dentition is present. Permanent tooth germs are visible within the alveolar bone in appropriate stages of calcification and eruption. Roots of erupting permanent teeth show incomplete formation (open apices), which is normal. Physiological root resorption of primary teeth is evident where permanent successors are erupting. No evidence of supernumerary teeth or congenitally missing teeth (hypodontia). No dental caries, periapical pathology, or cystic lesions. The mandibular canal and maxillary sinuses are normal for the developmental stage. TMJ condyles show normal growth morphology.

results.male_result: Sinus pneumatization correlates with developmental age. Maxillary and ethmoid sinuses are present and demonstrate normal translucency. Frontal sinuses may be absent (normal in young children under 6-8 years) or partially developed. Sphenoid sinuses show age-appropriate pneumatization. No evidence of mucosal thickening, air-fluid levels, or pathological opacification. Sinus walls are intact. Nasal septum is midline. On lateral view, nasopharyngeal soft tissue (adenoids) may be prominent, which is a common finding in this age group.

results.male_result: Bony pelvis demonstrates normal age-appropriate ossification and skeletal maturity. The triradiate cartilages (Y-cartilages) of the acetabula are open and symmetric (unless the child is nearing skeletal maturity). Femoral capital epiphyses are well-formed, spherical, and congruently seated within the acetabula. Shenton's lines are smooth and continuous bilaterally. The sacroiliac joints are symmetric. No evidence of fractures, dislocations, or lytic/sclerotic lesions. Soft tissues are unremarkable.
results.female_result: Bony pelvis demonstrates normal age-appropriate ossification and skeletal maturity. The triradiate cartilages are open and symmetric. While the pelvis may begin to show early female morphological characteristics in older children, distinct sexual dimorphism is less pronounced compared to adults. No fractures, dislocations, or abnormalities in ossification centers identified.

results.male_result: Soft tissue shadow of the penis is normal in contour and density, with size and development appropriate for the child's age and Tanner stage. No calcifications, foreign bodies, or osseous abnormalities are identified. The corpora cavernosa and corpus spongiosum demonstrate homogeneous soft tissue density. No evidence of congenital structural anomalies visible on plain film.

results.male_result: Normal periapical radiograph findings depend on the stage of dental development (deciduous, mixed, or early permanent dentition). Key features include: 1. Presence of primary and/or permanent teeth; 2. Physiological root resorption of primary teeth is normal when associated with the eruption of underlying permanent successors; 3. Developing permanent tooth germs (follicles) appear as well-defined radiolucent areas surrounding developing crowns within the alveolar bone; 4. Young permanent teeth exhibit large pulp chambers and wide root canals with open apices (incomplete root formation); 5. The periodontal ligament space may appear wider in erupting teeth compared to adults; 6. Lamina dura is continuous around roots not undergoing physiological resorption.

results.male_result: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in position and shape. In neonates and infants, the glands are relatively larger in proportion to the kidneys (approximately 1/3 of the kidney size) compared to adults. In older children, the glands resemble miniature adult adrenals with an inverted V (right) or inverted Y/triangular (left) configuration. Adrenal limbs are slender, typically measuring 2-6 mm in thickness depending on age. The contours are smooth with homogeneous soft tissue attenuation. No focal masses, calcifications, or hemorrhage are identified. Surrounding retroperitoneal fat planes are clear.

results.item_name: Brain and Orbital CT Scan (Brain/Orbital CT)
results.male_result: Brain: Bilateral cerebral hemispheres are symmetrical with gray-white matter differentiation appropriate for the child's developmental age (myelination patterns vary in infants). No evidence of intracranial hemorrhage, hypoxic-ischemic injury, or mass lesion. Ventricular system is normal in size and configuration without midline shift. Basal cisterns are patent. results.male_result: In neonates, the adrenal glands are proportionally larger relative to the kidneys (approximate ratio 1:3) compared to older children and adults, often appearing with a convex margin due to the presence of the fetal cortex. Following the involution of the fetal cortex (typically within the first 2-6 months of life), the glands assume the characteristic adult shape: the right gland is an inverted V or linear shape, and the left gland is an inverted Y or triangular shape. In infants and children, the absolute size is significantly smaller than in adults; limb thickness is typically very thin (often 2-5 mm depending on age, much less than the 10 mm adult limit). Signal intensity on T1 and T2 weighted images is generally isointense to slightly hypointense compared to the liver/spleen. No focal lesions or masses. Chemical shift imaging demonstrates normal signal drop in keeping with intracellular lipid content.

results.male_result: Liver: Normal size (strictly correlates with age and height), smooth contour, homogeneous parenchymal echogenicity. Portal vein diameter is age-dependent (smaller than adult 13mm cutoff). Gallbladder: Normal size for age, wall thin, no stones. Spleen: Normal size (length correlates with age/height). Pancreas: Size increases with age, homogeneous echogenicity. Kidneys: Length is highly age-dependent (e.g., infant ~4-5cm); in neonates and young infants, renal cortical echogenicity is normally higher (isoechoic to liver) compared to adults, and medullary pyramids are prominent. Bladder: Normal wall thickness. Prostate: In prepubertal boys, the tissue is rudimentary/small and often not distinct; dimensions increase significantly during puberty.

Bladder: Normal wall thickness. Uterus: In neonates, may be transiently enlarged due to maternal hormones. In prepubertal girls, the uterus is small and tubular shape with the cervix comprising a larger proportion than the corpus (cervix:corpus ratio ~2:1 or 1:1), and the endometrial line is typically not visible. In pubertal girls, the uterus becomes pear-shaped (corpus > cervix) with visible endometrial phases. Ovaries: Prepubertal ovaries are small (<1-2ml) and may contain small microcysts; functional cysts/follicles appear with puberty.

results.male_result: The brachial plexus nerve roots (C5-T1), trunks, divisions, and cords are visualized with normal echogenicity. The nerves appear as hypoechoic oval or round structures; the internal fascicular pattern may be less distinct in infants compared to adults but becomes clearer in older children. Nerve dimensions (diameter and Cross-Sectional Area) are significantly smaller than adult values and correlate positively with age, height, and weight. For example, mean CSA for C5-C7 roots in young children typically ranges between 2-5 mm² depending on age. The interscalene groove is clearly identified. No focal nerve enlargement, pseudomeningoceles, or discontinuity. Normal sliding with passive movement (if feasible). Subclavian vessels show normal flow.

No pathologic masses, complex cysts, or architectural distortion. Axillary lymph nodes are normal in size and morphology.

results.male_result: Bilateral common carotid arteries (CCA), internal carotid arteries (ICA), and external carotid arteries (ECA) demonstrate normal course and caliber appropriate for age. Intima-media thickness (IMT) is within normal limits for pediatric age groups (typically < 0.5 mm). No evidence of arterial wall thickening, congenital anomalies, dissection, or aneurysms. Color Doppler shows normal laminar flow. Flow velocities are generally higher than in adults due to smaller vessel diameter and higher cardiac output. Peak systolic velocity (PSV) in CCA typically ranges from 80-120 cm/s; PSV in ICA typically ranges from 90-140 cm/s, decreasing towards adult values as age increases. Low-resistance spectral waveforms observed in ICA. Vertebral arteries show normal antegrade flow.

results.male_result: Liver: Homogeneous enhancement pattern. Due to faster physiological circulation times in children, the arterial phase onset and peak enhancement occur significantly earlier than in adults (often starting <10-15 seconds), followed by iso-enhancement in the portal venous and late phases. Kidneys: Symmetric cortical enhancement with rapid arterial phase uptake and subsequent medullary enhancement. Spleen: Homogeneous enhancement. Major vessels: Patent with normal flow. No focal lesions or abnormal wash-out patterns detected.

results.male_result: Standard anatomical cranial ultrasound is generally not applicable as the anterior fontanelle is typically closed by 12-18 months, preventing acoustic penetration. If Transcranial Doppler (TCD) is performed, blood flow velocities are age-dependent and generally higher than in adults. In children (ages 2-10), Middle Cerebral Artery (MCA) mean velocities normally range from 70 to 110 cm/s (peaking around age 4-6), gradually decreasing during adolescence toward adult values. Flow should be symmetric bilaterally without evidence of focal acceleration or turbulence.

results.male_result: Arterial system: Patent vessels with laminar flow. Peripheral arteries demonstrate normal high-resistance (triphasic or biphasic) waveforms appropriate for age. Visceral arteries (e.g., renal, hepatic) demonstrate low-resistance flow. Renal Resistive Index (RI) is age-dependent: neonates and young infants often exhibit higher RI (up to 0.80), which typically decreases to adult levels (<0.70) in older children. Peak Systolic Velocities (PSV) vary significantly by age and vessel diameter. Venous system: Patent, compressible veins with spontaneous phasic flow; no evidence of thrombosis. Carotid arteries: Intima-media thickness is very thin (typically <0.5 mm), with no evidence of plaque or stenosis.

Carotid arteries: Normal thin intima-media thickness, no plaque. Pelvic vessels (if assessed): In pre-pubertal girls, uterine and ovarian arteries typically show a high-resistance pattern with low velocity and minimal diastolic flow, distinctive from the post-pubertal adult pattern.

results.male_result: Cardiac chamber dimensions (LVEDD, LVESD, LAD, RVD) and wall thicknesses (IVS, LVPW) are strictly dependent on Age and Body Surface Area (BSA); absolute values are significantly smaller than adults and must be evaluated using Z-scores (normal range -2 to +2 SD). Left Ventricular Ejection Fraction (LVEF) typically 56-78% (often slightly higher than adults). Normal cardiac situs (levocardia). No congenital heart defects (e.g., ASD, VSD, PDA, Coarctation) observed. All valves thin, pliable, and competent. Normal systemic and pulmonary venous connections. Coronary arteries originate normally from the appropriate sinuses.

results.male_result: Thyroid: Lobes are appropriate in volume for age and height, homogeneous parenchymal echotexture, no focal lesions. results.male_result: Muscles: Normal echogenicity with characteristic fiber architecture, though muscle bulk is naturally smaller than in adults. Tendons and Ligaments: Normal fibrillar echotexture; insertions (entheses) frequently attach to cartilaginous apophyses. Joints: Thicker hypoechoic hyaline cartilage compared to adults. Epiphyses (secondary ossification centers) may appear completely hypoechoic (cartilaginous) or partially ossified depending on age. No joint effusion or synovial thickening.

results.male_result: Urinary bladder: Well-distended with anechoic lumen, smooth wall, wall thickness <3mm when distended. Ovaries: Prepubertal volume is small (typically <2 mL), though small follicles (<9mm) are frequently seen. Ovarian volume increases with puberty. No adnexal masses. results.male_result: No pleural effusion detected. Normal visceral and parietal pleura appearing as a single hyperechoic line (pleural line) with smooth, regular contour. No pleural thickening, nodules, or masses. Normal diaphragmatic excursion with respiration. In infants and young children, the normal thymus may be visible in the anterior upper chest (appearing as a hypoechoic structure similar to liver texture) and should not be mistaken for a mass or effusion.

results.male_result: Both kidneys are normal in size, shape, and position, consistent with the child's age and height. Kidney length varies significantly by developmental stage (approx. 4-5 cm in neonates, increasing to adult size by late adolescence); size should be interpreted using age/height-based nomograms. In infants (0-6 months), the renal cortex may be isoechoic to the liver/spleen, becoming hypoechoic in older children. Medullary pyramids are typically prominent and hypoechoic in infants (distinct corticomedullary differentiation). The central renal sinus echoes are less prominent in infants due to scant sinus fat, becoming hyperechoic with age. Fetal lobulation (smooth indentations) may be present. No hydronephrosis, calculi, cysts, or masses. Doppler RI may be higher in neonates (up to 0.8) before settling to adult levels (<0.7) after the first year.

results.male_result: Normal cardiac anatomy with situs solitus, levocardia, and normal atrioventricular and ventriculoarterial connections. Left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) is generally >55% (range 55-75%). Right ventricular function is normal. Ventricular walls show normal thickness and contractility. All valves (mitral, tricuspid, aortic, pulmonary) are morphologically normal without stenosis or significant regurgitation. Interatrial and interventricular septa are intact with no evidence of congenital defects (e.g., ASD, VSD). No pericardial effusion. Coronary artery origins are normal.

Cardiac chamber volumes (LVEDV, LVESV, RVEDV) and mass are appropriate for body surface area (BSA) and age, with Z-scores typically between -2 and +2. Left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) is generally >55% (range 55-75%). Right ventricular function is normal. Ventricular walls show normal thickness and contractility. All valves (mitral, tricuspid, aortic, pulmonary) are morphologically normal without stenosis or significant regurgitation. Interatrial and interventricular septa are intact with no evidence of congenital defects (e.g., ASD, VSD). No pericardial effusion. Coronary artery origins are normal.

results.male_result: Not applicable
results.female_result: Not applicable. This examination is generally not performed on children. Physiologically, a pre-pubertal female's uterus is infantile (small, tubular, with a cervix-to-corpus ratio of 2:1 or 1:1) and ovaries are small without functional follicles. However, functional tubal patency testing is clinically irrelevant for this age group.

results.male_result: Thyroid gland is normal in position and morphology, with size and volume appropriate for age and body surface area. Both lobes are symmetric with smooth contours. The parenchyma demonstrates homogeneous echogenicity (usually hyperechoic relative to anterior strap muscles) without focal lesions, nodules, or cystic changes. Isthmus is thin and continuous. No calcifications identified. Normal vascularity on color Doppler imaging without increased blood flow. No pathological cervical lymphadenopathy. Absolute dimensions are significantly smaller than adults and increase progressively with age.

No calcifications identified. Normal vascularity on color Doppler imaging without increased blood flow. No pathological cervical lymphadenopathy. Absolute dimensions are significantly smaller than adults and increase progressively with age.

results.male_result: Bilateral middle cerebral arteries (MCA), anterior cerebral arteries (ACA), and posterior cerebral arteries (PCA) demonstrate normal flow velocities and waveform patterns. Flow velocities are significantly higher than in adults and are strongly age-dependent. Velocities typically increase after birth, peak between 3 to 6 years of age (with MCA mean velocities often ranging 90-110 cm/s), and gradually decline toward adult levels during adolescence. Low resistance flow patterns are maintained. No focal stenosis, occlusion, or hemodynamically significant abnormalities detected. Symmetric flow velocities bilaterally.
results.female_result: Bilateral middle cerebral arteries (MCA), anterior cerebral arteries (ACA), and posterior cerebral arteries (PCA) demonstrate normal flow velocities and waveform patterns. Flow velocities are significantly higher than in adults and are strongly age-dependent. Velocities typically increase after birth, peak between 3 to 6 years of age (with MCA mean velocities often ranging 90-110 cm/s), and gradually decline toward adult levels during adolescence. Low resistance flow patterns are maintained. No focal stenosis, occlusion, or hemodynamically significant abnormalities detected. Symmetric flow velocities bilaterally.

results.male_result: Cardiac chamber dimensions (LA, LV, RA, RV) and wall thicknesses are within normal limits relative to Body Surface Area (BSA), with Z-scores typically between -2 and +2. Left ventricular systolic function is normal (LVEF >55%, Fractional Shortening 28-44%). All cardiac valves are thin, pliable, with normal motion and no significant stenosis or regurgitation; valve annulus sizes are appropriate for somatic growth. The interatrial and interventricular septa are intact with no evidence of ASD or VSD (unless monitoring closure). Aortic root and thoracic aorta dimensions are normal for BSA. No pericardial effusion. Pulmonary venous return is normal. Left atrial appendage is free of thrombus.
