results.male_result: Liver stiffness measurement (LSM): typically 2.5-6.0 kPa; Controlled Attenuation Parameter (CAP): < 225-240 dB/m (if available on pediatric software); No significant hepatic fibrosis; Normal liver parenchyma elasticity; Valid measurements generally require success rate ≥60% and IQR/median ≤30%, though variability is higher in younger children.

results.male_result: The prostate gland is significantly smaller and less developed compared to adults. In prepubertal boys, the prostate is rudimentary, typically weighing less than 5 grams (volume < 5 mL), and appears as a small, hypoechoic structure without distinct zonal differentiation (peripheral, central, transition zones are not defined). The seminal vesicles are small and may be difficult to visualize. During adolescence (puberty), the prostate volume increases rapidly, and echogenicity changes to become more characteristic of adult anatomy.
results.female_result: Not applicable as a routine examination. Transrectal ultrasound is rarely performed in pediatric females; transabdominal ultrasound is the standard for pelvic evaluation. If performed (e.g., for specific pelvic pathology where transabdominal views are poor and transvaginal is contraindicated), normal findings include an intact rectal wall and age-appropriate size and morphology of the uterus and ovaries (e.g., small, tubular uterus in prepubertal girls).

results.male_result: Cardiac chamber dimensions (LVEDD, LVESD, LA, RV, Aortic root) and wall thicknesses (IVS, LVPW) are strictly dependent on Body Surface Area (BSA). Absolute values must be interpreted using Z-scores (normal range typically -2 to +2 SD). Normal systolic function: Left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) 55-75%, Fractional Shortening (FS) 28-45%. Normal global and regional wall motion. Valves: thin, pliable, no stenosis or regurgitation. Situs solitus, levocardia. No evidence of congenital heart defects (e.g., no ASD, VSD, PDA, Tetralogy of Fallot). Normal systemic and pulmonary venous return. Normal aortic arch anatomy with no evidence of coarctation.

results.male_result: Not applicable
results.female_result: Contraindicated. This specific examination method (Transvaginal) is not performed on pediatric patients (children/virgins) due to the intact hymen and small vaginal canal. Pelvic assessment in this age group is performed via Transabdominal Ultrasound. Normal findings for pediatric pelvic anatomy (via abdominal route): Pre-pubertal uterus is small (length 2.5-4 cm), tubular in shape, with a cervix-to-fundus ratio of approximately 2:1 (cervix larger than fundus). Endometrial stripe is typically thin or not visible. Ovaries are small (volume < 1-2 cm³) and may contain small micro-follicles.

results.male_result: Liver: Homogeneous parenchymal echotexture, smooth contour, size appropriate for age and height. Gallbladder: Thin wall, anechoic lumen, no calculi. Spleen: Homogeneous echotexture, length correlates with age/height. Pancreas: Normal echogenicity (may appear hypoechoic in younger children compared to adults), size appropriate for age. Kidneys: Size appropriate for age/height, distinct corticomedullary differentiation (fetal lobulation may persist in young children). Bladder: Smooth wall, anechoic content. Prostate: Small, prepubertal size (often difficult to distinguish or appears as a small tissue thickening at the bladder base), volume significantly less than adults. Aorta: Caliber appropriate for body surface area.
results.female_result: Liver: Homogeneous parenchymal echotexture, smooth contour, size appropriate for age and height. Gallbladder: Thin wall, anechoic lumen, no calculi. Spleen: Homogeneous echotexture, length correlates with age/height. Pancreas: Normal echogenicity, size appropriate for age. Kidneys: Size appropriate for age/height, distinct corticomedullary differentiation. Bladder: Smooth wall, anechoic content. Uterus: Prepubertal morphology (tubular shape, cervix may be larger than or equal to fundus size in infants/young children), small size, thin linear endometrium. Ovaries: Small volume (usually <1-2 cc in prepuberty), may contain small microcysts, no dominant follicles. Aorta: Caliber appropriate for body surface area.

results.male_result: Liver: Normal size commensurate with age and body surface area, smooth contour, homogeneous parenchymal echogenicity. Gallbladder: Normal size for age, wall thickness <3mm. Kidneys: Normal size for age (length correlates with height), smooth contour, distinct corticomedullary differentiation. Bladder: Normal wall thickness. results.male_result: Cornea: Central corneal thickness reaches adult-like values (approx. 520-560 μm) by age 2-3 years; uniform echogenicity. Anterior chamber: Depth is age-dependent, generally shallower in infants and young children (often < 2.5 mm in newborns, increasing to adult range of 2.5-3.5 mm by school age); optically clear. Anterior chamber angle: Open angle configuration; typically examined to rule out congenital dysgenesis (e.g., in congenital glaucoma). Iris: Uniform thickness, smooth surfaces. Ciliary body: Normal configuration, though the pars plana is anatomically shorter in infants compared to adults. Lens: Anterior capsule intact; lens shape is more spherical and thicker anteroposteriorly in infants/young children compared to adults due to active accommodation and growth.

Anterior chamber angle: Open angle configuration; typically examined to rule out congenital dysgenesis (e.g., in congenital glaucoma). Iris: Uniform thickness, smooth surfaces. results.male_result: Liver stiffness measurement (LSM): 2.5-6.5 kPa (median values in healthy children are often slightly lower than adults, typically around 4.0-5.0 kPa). Thyroid nodules: Elasticity Score 1-2 (soft, benign pattern). Prostate: Generally not applicable for routine pediatric examination; tissue should be soft if examined. Musculoskeletal: Homogeneous elasticity pattern with normal tissue compliance. Kidney: Cortical stiffness 2.0-5.0 kPa. Spleen stiffness: 10.0-22.0 kPa (age-dependent). Overall tissue demonstrates uniform color distribution on elastogram (typically blue/green indicating soft tissue), with symmetric strain patterns.
results.female_result: Liver stiffness measurement (LSM): 2.5-6.5 kPa (median values typically around 4.0-5.0 kPa). Thyroid nodules: Elasticity Score 1-2 (soft, benign pattern). Breast tissue: Pre-pubertal breast tissue is rudimentary; Adolescent breast tissue: Strain ratio <3.0, Elasticity Score 1-2 (soft, homogeneous pattern), shear wave velocity <3.0 m/s. Musculoskeletal: Homogeneous elasticity pattern with normal tissue compliance. Kidney: Cortical stiffness 2.0-5.0 kPa. Spleen stiffness: 10.0-22.0 kPa (age-dependent). results.male_result: The urethra demonstrates normal caliber and wall thickness proportionate to the child's age and developmental stage. The posterior urethra shows no evidence of dilation, ruling out posterior urethral valves (no 'keyhole' sign). The prostate appears small and hypoechoic, consistent with prepubertal anatomy (if applicable). The anterior urethra is patent with uniform diameter, showing no strictures, diverticula, or cobweb-like membranes. No calculi or foreign bodies are identified.
results.female_result: The urethra appears patent with dimensions (length and diameter) appropriate for the child's age, being significantly shorter and narrower than in adults. results.male_result: Kidneys: Bilateral kidneys are normal in position and shape with size appropriate for the child's age and height (renal length increases with somatic growth). In neonates and young infants (up to 6 months), renal cortical echogenicity may be isoechoic to the liver or spleen; in older children, it becomes hypoechoic relative to the liver. Medullary pyramids are typically prominent and hypoechoic with distinct corticomedullary differentiation. No hydronephrosis, calculi, cysts, or masses identified. Fetal lobulation may be present (normal variant). Ureters: Not dilated. Bladder: Bladder wall is smooth and thin when distended. No intraluminal masses. Prostate: In prepubertal boys, the prostate is rudimentary, small, and often not clearly differentiated from the bladder base; in adolescents, it begins to enlarge but volume is age-dependent.

results.male_result: Carotid arteries: Bilateral common, internal, and external carotid arteries demonstrate normal caliber proportionate to age and body surface area. Vessel walls are smooth and thin; intima-media thickness (IMT) is minimal and uniform. Flow velocities are age-dependent, typically higher than adult values (CCA PSV often > 100 cm/s in infants and young children, decreasing toward adult values in adolescence). Vertebral arteries: Antegrade flow. Lower extremity arteries: Patent vessels with smooth walls, normal multiphasic/triphasic waveforms, no evidence of congenital stenosis or occlusion. Lower extremity veins: Deep veins are fully compressible, demonstrate phasicity with respiration (respiratory rate is faster in children), no thrombus. Abdominal aorta: Normal diameter relative to body size with regular tapering, no evidence of hypoplasia, coarctation, or dilatation.

Venous system: Veins are fully compressible with normal respiratory phasicity. No intraluminal thrombus or echogenic material identified. Normal venous flow direction without significant reflux. results.male_result: Symmetric radiotracer uptake throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton. The most distinct normal feature is intense, symmetric, linear uptake at the epiphyseal growth plates (physes) of long bones, particularly prominent at the distal femora, proximal tibiae, proximal humeri, and distal radii/ulnae. Costochondral junctions may show prominent focal uptake ('rosary' appearance). The skull shows uniform distribution, though sutures may be prominent in infants. Kidneys are visualized bilaterally with normal excretion into the bladder. No focal asymmetric abnormalities suggestive of fracture, osteomyelitis, or malignancy.
results.female_result: Symmetric radiotracer uptake throughout the axial and appendicular skeleton. results.male_result: Early imaging (15-30 minutes): Homogeneous myocardial uptake of I-123 MIBG with clear cardiac silhouette. Delayed imaging (3-4 hours): Maintained myocardial uptake. Heart-to-Mediastinum ratio (H/M ratio): In healthy children, sympathetic innervation is robust; values are typically comparable to or higher than healthy adults (Early H/M ≥ 1.9-2.0, Delayed H/M ≥ 2.0-2.2). Washout rate (WR): < 25-30%. CAUTION: Presence of physiological brown adipose tissue in the mediastinum may artificially lower the H/M ratio by increasing background activity.
results.female_result: Early imaging (15-30 minutes): Homogeneous myocardial uptake of I-123 MIBG with clear cardiac silhouette. Delayed imaging (3-4 hours): Maintained myocardial uptake. Washout rate (WR): < 25-30%. CAUTION: Presence of physiological brown adipose tissue in the mediastinum may artificially lower the H/M ratio by increasing background activity.

results.male_result: Normal values are highly dependent on age and the type of meal administered (solid vs. liquid). For older children and adolescents capable of consuming the standard solid meal, adult reference values apply: Gastric retention at 1 hour: ≤90%; 2 hours: ≤60%; 3 hours: ≤30%; 4 hours: ≤10%; T1/2 (half-emptying time): 60-90 minutes. For younger children or those consuming liquid/formula meals: Emptying is significantly faster, with a normal T1/2 typically between 30-60 minutes. The radiotracer should show smooth, progressive emptying from the stomach to the small bowel without obstruction.
results.female_result: Normal values are highly dependent on age and the type of meal administered (solid vs. liquid). For older children and adolescents capable of consuming the standard solid meal, adult reference values apply: Gastric retention at 1 hour: ≤90%; 2 hours: ≤60%; 3 hours: ≤30%; 4 hours: ≤10%; T1/2 (half-emptying time): 60-90 minutes. For younger children or those consuming liquid/formula meals: Emptying is significantly faster, with a normal T1/2 typically between 30-60 minutes. results.male_result: Radiotracer (Tc-99m IDA derivative) demonstrates prompt hepatic uptake within 5 minutes of injection. Homogeneous hepatic parenchymal activity with progressive clearance. Biliary tree visualization: intrahepatic ducts, common hepatic duct, and common bile duct visualized within 15-30 minutes. Gallbladder visualization within 30-60 minutes post-injection, demonstrating normal filling and morphology. Radiotracer transit into duodenum observed within 60 minutes. Gallbladder ejection fraction (GBEF) ≥35% following CCK or fatty meal stimulation. No evidence of focal hepatic defects, biliary obstruction, or radiotracer extravasation. Hepatic clearance half-time <15 minutes.

results.male_result: Homogeneous distribution of radiotracer throughout the cardiac blood pool with clear delineation of cardiac chambers. Normal left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) in children is generally higher than adults, typically ranging from 55% to 75%. No evidence of abnormal radiotracer accumulation or pooling in the gastrointestinal tract. Spleen and liver demonstrate normal physiological uptake relative to the child's age. No focal areas of increased activity suggesting active hemorrhage, infantile hepatic hemangioma, or other vascular malformations. Major blood vessels show symmetric tracer distribution. No extravascular tracer accumulation.
results.female_result: Homogeneous distribution of radiotracer throughout the cardiac blood pool with clear delineation of cardiac chambers. results.male_result: Ventilation images: Homogeneous radiotracer distribution throughout both lung fields with no focal defects. Perfusion images: Uniform radiotracer uptake bilaterally with no segmental, subsegmental, or lobar perfusion defects. V/Q match: Complete concordance between ventilation and perfusion images with no mismatched defects. Normal gradient of increased perfusion to dependent lung zones (often posterior in supine infants/children). No evidence of right-to-left shunt (kidneys/brain free of tracer).
results.female_result: Ventilation images: Homogeneous radiotracer distribution throughout both lung fields with no focal defects. Perfusion images: Uniform radiotracer uptake bilaterally with no segmental, subsegmental, or lobar perfusion defects. V/Q match: Complete concordance between ventilation and perfusion images with no mismatched defects. Normal gradient of increased perfusion to dependent lung zones. No evidence of right-to-left shunt. (Note: Breast attenuation artifacts seen in adults are typically absent in prepubertal females).

results.male_result: Symmetric visualization of lymphatic channels with prompt radiotracer uptake. Normal drainage pattern from the injection site to regional lymph nodes is observed. Transit time to regional nodes is often rapid in children, typically within 10-40 minutes depending on the injection site and physical activity. Sentinel nodes or regional node basins (inguinal, axillary) are clearly identified with homogeneous tracer accumulation. There is no evidence of lymphatic obstruction, dermal backflow (indicative of reflux), or recruitment of collateral lymphatic pathways. No asymmetric tracer retention or delayed clearance is noted.
results.female_result: Symmetric visualization of lymphatic channels with prompt radiotracer uptake. Normal drainage pattern from the injection site to regional lymph nodes is observed. There is no evidence of lymphatic obstruction, dermal backflow (indicative of reflux), or recruitment of collateral lymphatic pathways. No asymmetric tracer retention or delayed clearance is noted.

results.male_result: Normal physiological uptake is observed in the salivary glands, myocardium, liver, spleen, gut, and urinary bladder. A notable normal variant in children is the visualization of Brown Adipose Tissue (BAT), often seen as symmetric uptake in the supraclavicular, cervical, axillary, mediastinal, and paraspinal regions. Adrenal glands typically show faint to no visualization. No abnormal focal high-intensity uptake suggestive of neuroblastoma (most common pediatric indication), pheochromocytoma, or paraganglioma. Skeletal background activity is generally low and symmetric.
results.female_result: Normal physiological uptake is observed in the salivary glands, myocardium, liver, spleen, gut, and urinary bladder. results.male_result: Homogeneous radiotracer uptake throughout the left ventricular myocardium. No fixed or reversible perfusion defects. Normal left ventricular wall motion and thickening. Left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) is generally ≥55% (often higher than adults). In neonates and young infants, prominent right ventricular uptake may be observed and is considered physiological due to developmental right ventricular dominance. No evidence of stress-induced ischemia. No extracardiac abnormal uptake.
results.female_result: Homogeneous radiotracer uptake throughout the left ventricular myocardium. No fixed or reversible perfusion defects. Normal left ventricular wall motion and thickening. Left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) is generally ≥55%. In neonates and young infants, prominent right ventricular uptake is physiological. Unlike adult women, prepubescent females typically do not exhibit breast attenuation artifacts. No evidence of stress-induced ischemia. No extracardiac abnormal uptake.

results.male_result: No significant cortical Pittsburgh Compound B (PIB) retention. Visual assessment demonstrates no focal or regional increased tracer binding in the frontal, parietal, temporal, or occipital cortices. Only diffuse non-specific white matter uptake is observed. The pattern is consistent with the absence of beta-amyloid deposition (amyloid-negative status).
results.female_result: No significant cortical Pittsburgh Compound B (PIB) retention. Visual assessment demonstrates no focal or regional increased tracer binding in the frontal, parietal, temporal, or occipital cortices. Only diffuse non-specific white matter uptake is observed. The pattern is consistent with the absence of beta-amyloid deposition (amyloid-negative status).

results.male_result: No abnormal focal areas of increased radiotracer uptake. Physiologic uptake observed in brain, heart, liver, and urinary tract. Distinct normal physiologic uptake is typically visualized in the thymus gland. Intense, symmetric radiotracer uptake is present in the epiphyseal growth plates of long bones, corresponding to active skeletal growth. Physiologic uptake in brown adipose tissue (neck, supraclavicular, mediastinal, and paravertebral regions) is frequently observed. No evidence of hypermetabolic lesions suggestive of malignancy.
results.female_result: No abnormal focal areas of increased radiotracer uptake. Physiologic uptake observed in brain, heart, liver, and urinary tract. Intense, symmetric radiotracer uptake is present in the epiphyseal growth plates of long bones, corresponding to active skeletal growth. Physiologic uptake in brown adipose tissue (neck, supraclavicular, mediastinal, and paravertebral regions) is frequently observed. No evidence of hypermetabolic lesions suggestive of malignancy.

results.male_result: No abnormal hypermetabolic foci identified suspicious for malignancy. Brain demonstrates high physiologic cortical and subcortical FDG uptake. Waldeyer's ring (adenoids and tonsils) frequently shows symmetric, prominent physiologic uptake due to reactive lymphoid tissue. The thymus gland often exhibits physiologic uptake (typically appearing as an 'inverted V' or quadrilateral shape in the anterior mediastinum), which is a normal finding in children and adolescents. Myocardium shows variable uptake. Liver and spleen demonstrate homogeneous background uptake. The skeleton displays characteristic intense, symmetric, linear physiologic uptake at the epiphyseal growth plates of long bones. Kidneys and urinary bladder demonstrate expected excretory activity. Brown adipose tissue (BAT) activation in the neck, supraclavicular, and paravertebral regions is more common than in adults. Testes demonstrate low-grade physiologic uptake.
results.female_result: No abnormal hypermetabolic foci identified suspicious for malignancy. Brain demonstrates high physiologic cortical and subcortical FDG uptake. Waldeyer's ring (adenoids and tonsils) frequently shows symmetric, prominent physiologic uptake due to reactive lymphoid tissue. The thymus gland often exhibits physiologic uptake in the anterior mediastinum, which is a normal finding in children and adolescents. Myocardium shows variable uptake. Liver and spleen demonstrate homogeneous background uptake. The skeleton displays characteristic intense, symmetric, linear physiologic uptake at the epiphyseal growth plates of long bones. Kidneys and urinary bladder demonstrate expected excretory activity. Brown adipose tissue (BAT) activation is more common than in adults. Pre-pubertal uterus and ovaries show minimal uptake; post-pubertal adolescents may demonstrate variable physiologic uptake depending on the menstrual cycle.

Split renal function shows balanced differential function (each kidney 45-55% of total). Normalized Glomerular filtration rate (GFR) using Tc-99m DTPA matches adult values (90-120 mL/min/1.73m²) in children over 2 years old. Normalized Effective renal plasma flow (ERPF) using Tc-99m MAG3 matches adult values (500-600 mL/min/1.73m²) in children over 2 years old.

results.male_result:
Corneal curvature varies significantly by developmental stage. 
1. Newborns/Infants (<1 year): Corneas are significantly steeper. Average central corneal power ranges from 47.00 to 54.00 D at birth, flattening rapidly during the first year.
2. Toddlers (1-3 years): Values approach adult ranges, typically averaging 44.00-47.00 D.
3. School-age/Adolescents: Values align with adult norms (Average central corneal power: 42.00-46.00 D; Anterior corneal curvature radius: 7.5-8.0 mm). 
General appearance: Smooth, regular corneal surface with symmetric bow-tie pattern. Corneal astigmatism may be higher in infants but typically decreases to < 1.50 D by school age. Surface Regularity Index (SRI) and Surface Asymmetry Index (SAI) should be low, similar to adults, provided fixation is stable.

Corneal endothelium: Regular hexagonal mosaic pattern is very distinct; endothelial cell density (ECD) is significantly higher than in adults, typically ranging from 3,500 to over 4,500 cells/mm² in infants and young children, gradually decreasing towards adult levels during adolescence; hexagonality is high and polymegethism is low.

results.male_result: Normal ICGA findings proceed through three distinct phases: 1. Early Phase (0-3 minutes): Rapid sequential filling of choroidal arteries, followed by the choriocapillaris flush, and finally choroidal veins. Large choroidal vessels appear hyperfluorescent (bright) as they fill with dye. 2. Mid Phase (3-15 minutes): Vessel margins become less distinct as the dye diffuses, resulting in a diffuse hyperfluorescence of the choroidal tissue. 3. Late Phase (15-30 minutes): The characteristic 'inversion phenomenon' is observed. Large choroidal vessels appear hypofluorescent (dark) silhouetted against a hyperfluorescent (bright) background caused by the staining of the choroidal stroma. The optic disc typically appears hypofluorescent in the late phase.

results.male_result: Spinal canal is of normal caliber for age throughout cervical, thoracic, and lumbar regions. Contrast column demonstrates smooth, uninterrupted flow with no filling defects. results.male_result: Capillary loops are arranged in regular rows, though density is often lower in younger children (typically 6-10 loops per millimeter) and increases towards adult levels with age. Loop morphology is predominantly hairpin-shaped, but minor tortuosity is more frequently observed and considered a normal variant in children. A characteristic normal finding in children is the high visibility of the subpapillary venous plexus due to thinner skin. No giant capillaries, hemorrhages, avascular areas, or bushy capillaries should be observed. Blood flow is continuous.

results.male_result: Retinal layers are well-defined and clearly delineated. Ganglion cell-inner plexiform layer (GC-IPL) thickness is preserved. Foveal contour is normal with intact foveal depression (though the pit deepens during early development). No pericardial thickening, calcification, or masses identified. No evidence of pericardial adhesions or constriction. results.male_result: Paranasal sinus pneumatization correlates with age. The ethmoid and maxillary sinuses are present at birth but are small and continue to grow. The sphenoid sinuses typically begin pneumatization around age 2-3. The frontal sinuses are usually not radiologically visible until age 6-8 and continue to develop into late adolescence. In the developed sinuses, air spaces are clear without opacification, mucosal thickening, or air-fluid levels. Prominent adenoidal soft tissue in the nasopharynx is a common normal finding in children.

results.male_result: Superior mesenteric artery (SMA) arises from the anterior aspect of the abdominal aorta, typically at the level of T12-L1. The vessel diameter is proportionate to the child's age and body surface area (significantly smaller than the adult 6-8 mm range, varying from approx. 2-3 mm in infants to adult sizes in adolescents). Normal branching pattern is observed with jejunal, ileal, ileocolic, right colic, and middle colic arteries. Smooth vessel walls without stenosis, occlusion, aneurysm, or congenital irregularity. Normal tapering of vessels toward the periphery. Arterial phase shows rapid opacification; circulation time is often faster than in adults due to higher heart rate. Capillary phase demonstrates homogeneous bowel wall blush. Venous phase shows normal drainage into the superior mesenteric vein without filling defects. No evidence of arteriovenous malformation, tumor blush, or extravasation.

results.male_result: Absolute ventricular volumes are significantly lower than adults and are strictly dependent on Body Surface Area (BSA). Normal End-Diastolic Volume Index (EDVI): 58-88 mL/m²; Normal End-Systolic Volume Index (ESVI): 20-35 mL/m²; Ejection Fraction (LVEF): Typically >60% (range 56-78%); Left Ventricular End-Diastolic Pressure (LVEDP): 5-12 mmHg. Imaging demonstrates normal ventricular situs, D-looping (normal morphology), absence of congenital defects (e.g., VSD, ASD), symmetrical wall motion, and competent valves.

results.male_result: Esophagus and Stomach: Five distinct wall layers visualized (mucosa, muscularis mucosae, submucosa, muscularis propria, serosa/adventitia); wall thickness is age-dependent but generally consistent with or slightly thinner than adult ranges. results.male_result: Esophagus: The esophageal mucosa is smooth, pink, and intact with a normal vascular pattern. Peristalsis is present and uniform. The mucosa of the cardia, fundus, body, and antrum appears normal with no signs of inflammation, atrophy, erosion, ulceration, or masses. The pylorus is patent and functions normally. Duodenum: The duodenal bulb is non-deformed with intact mucosa. The second portion of the duodenum reveals normal circular folds (valves of Kerckring). The major papilla (ampulla of Vater) appears normal. No ulcers, masses, or diverticula are seen.

results.male_result: Articular cartilage: Thicker than in adults, smooth, glistening, with a bluish-white or pearly appearance; growth plates (physes) may be visible as distinct lines or zones near the femoral or tibial surfaces depending on skeletal maturity. Anterior cruciate ligament (ACL): Intact, highly taut, with healthy synovial covering and excellent tension. Posterior cruciate ligament (PCL): Intact with normal appearance and tension. Synovium: Thin, pink, healthy vascular membrane without hypertrophy or inflammation. Patella: Normal tracking, smooth articular surface; ossification may be incomplete depending on age. Trochlear groove: Smooth cartilage surface without defects. Joint capsule: No loose bodies, no adhesions, no evidence of synovitis or effusion.

results.male_result: EMG: No spontaneous activity at rest. Motor unit action potentials (MUAPs) typically have shorter duration (mean 5-10 ms) and lower amplitude in infants compared to adults, increasing with age. Recruitment pattern shows full interference on maximal contraction, though assessment depends on child cooperation. NCS: Nerve conduction velocities (NCV) are age-dependent due to incomplete myelination. In full-term newborns, NCV is approx. 50% of adult values (20-30 m/s). NCV reaches ~75% of adult values by 1 year and adult levels (>50 m/s upper, >40 m/s lower) by age 3-5. Distal latencies are shorter due to shorter limb length. CMAP and SNAP amplitudes are generally lower in infants due to smaller muscle mass and nerve caliber, increasing with growth.

results.male_result: Results are highly dependent on age and maturation of the oculomotor system. Saccade test: Latency is typically longer in young children (can be >250 ms) and reaches adult levels by adolescence; velocity is similar to adults. Smooth pursuit: Gain is often lower (0.6-0.9) in children <12 years; 'catch-up' saccades are more common and considered normal in younger children. Optokinetic nystagmus: Symmetric responses expected, though gain is lower in infants. Gaze/Positional: No spontaneous nystagmus; minor end-point nystagmus is common. Caloric test: Symmetry criteria remain similar (Unilateral weakness <20-25%, Directional preponderance <30%), but absolute Slow Phase Velocity (SPV) is often higher (hyperactive) in children compared to adults.

results.male_result: Results vary significantly by developmental stage. Neonates/Infants (<1 year): Amplitudes are significantly lower (initially 20-50% of adult mean) and implicit times are prolonged; Photopic responses mature faster than scotopic. Children/Adolescents (3-18 years): Amplitudes are typically equal to or higher than adult values due to clear ocular media and peak photoreceptor density (e.g., Dark-adapted b-wave often 300-650 μV; Light-adapted b-wave 120-250 μV). Implicit times in children >1 year match adult standards (Dark-adapted b-wave 80-110 ms, Light-adapted 28-35 ms).

results.male_result: SEP latencies in children are not static and change significantly with development. In neonates and infants (< 2 years), absolute latencies may be prolonged relative to body size due to incomplete myelination (slower conduction velocity). By age 3-5, conduction velocities reach adult levels. Consequently, in children aged 3 and older, absolute latencies are generally shorter than in adults due to shorter limb length and height, increasing linearly as the child grows. Specific normal values must be calculated using age-specific and height-based nomograms or regression equations rather than fixed ranges.
results.female_result: Similar to males, SEP latencies in female children are strictly dependent on age and height. While adult females often have slightly shorter latencies than males due to height differences, prepubescent children show minimal sex-based differences. Absolute latencies are generally shorter than standard adult values due to smaller body stature. Results should be interpreted by comparing the measured latency against height-matched normative data specific to the pediatric population.

results.male_result: Values are significantly age-dependent due to the maturation of the retina and optic nerve. Electroretinogram (ERG): In infants, a-wave and b-wave amplitudes start low (approx. 30-50% of adult values at birth) and increase rapidly, typically reaching adult levels by 1-2 years of age. Implicit times are slightly prolonged in neonates but normalize quickly. Visual Evoked Potential (VEP): Latencies are significantly longer in infants due to incomplete myelination. Flash VEP is often used; P2 latency decreases from ~120-170 ms at birth to near-adult levels by age 2. Pattern VEP P100 latency decreases from >200 ms in neonates to adult levels (approx. 100 ms) by age 3-5 years.

Implicit times are slightly prolonged in neonates but normalize quickly. Visual Evoked Potential (VEP): Latencies are significantly longer in infants due to incomplete myelination. Pattern VEP P100 latency decreases from >200 ms in neonates to adult levels (approx. 100 ms) by age 3-5 years.

results.male_result: Values are strictly age-dependent. Infants (Flash VEP): P2 latency is prolonged (110-170 ms) at birth, rapidly decreasing in the first year. Children (Pattern VEP): P100 latency decreases from ~110 ms in toddlers to adult levels (approx. 100 ms) by age 5-7 years. Amplitude: Often higher than adults (10-40 μV) due to higher skull conductivity. Interocular latency difference: <10 ms.

results.male_result: Normal bone architecture with intact trabecular pattern. Bone marrow cellularity is significantly higher than in adults, typically ranging from 80-100% in infants and gradually decreasing to 60-80% in older children. Normal trilineage hematopoiesis is present. A significant population of hematogones (benign precursor B-cells) is a common and normal finding, particularly in young children. Myeloid to erythroid ratio is typically 2:1 to 4:1. No evidence of fibrosis, necrosis, granulomas, or malignant infiltration. Osteoblasts and osteoclasts are present, potentially reflecting active skeletal growth.

results.male_result: Results are strictly dependent on the specific antibody clone and tissue tested. In normal, healthy pediatric tissue, findings include: 1. Appropriate Positive Staining: Native antigens appear in expected cell lineages with correct subcellular localization (e.g., Cytokeratins in epithelium; CD45 on leukocytes), generally mirroring adult patterns. 2. Negative Staining: Absence of aberrant expression (e.g., viral antigens, oncogenic proteins). 3. Proliferation: Markers like Ki-67 generally show low indices in resting tissues, but may show naturally higher physiological proliferation in tissues undergoing active somatic growth (e.g., epiphyseal plates) or in active lymphoid organs (e.g., hyperplastic tonsils) compared to adults.
results.female_result: Results are strictly dependent on the specific antibody clone and tissue tested. Generally same as male findings. Specific Note: Unlike adults, pre-pubertal females possess immature reproductive tissues. Consequently, the physiological expression patterns of hormone receptors (Estrogen Receptor [ER], Progesterone Receptor [PR]) in breast or endometrial tissue differ from adults and typically lack the heterogenous, cyclic intensity seen in reproductive-age women.

There is no evidence of acute inflammation (neutrophils), cryptitis, crypt abscesses, granulomas, architectural distortion, dysplasia, or malignancy.

results.male_result: Light microscopy: Normal glomerular architecture with patent capillary loops. Glomeruli are structurally intact but may appear smaller in volume in infants and young children compared to adults. Normal mesangial cellularity (typically <3 cells per mesangial area), no mesangial matrix expansion. Tubules show normal epithelial cells; proximal tubules have preserved brush borders. Interstitium is unremarkable without fibrosis or inflammation. Blood vessels show normal walls. Immunofluorescence: Negative for IgG, IgA, IgM, C3, C4, C1q, fibrinogen, kappa and lambda light chains. Electron microscopy: Glomerular basement membrane (GBM) thickness is age-dependent and significantly thinner than in adults. In young children (2-10 years), GBM thickness typically ranges from 200-280 nm, gradually increasing to adult values (300-400 nm) during adolescence. Normal trilaminar structure is maintained. Podocytes show intact foot processes without effacement. No electron-dense deposits.

Podocytes show intact foot processes without effacement. No electron-dense deposits.

results.male_result: Epidermis: Normal stratified squamous epithelium. Dermis: Normal collagen and elastic fiber architecture. Sebaceous glands: Small and undeveloped (quiescent) compared to adults, as they enlarge only after puberty. Hair follicles: Significantly higher follicular density compared to adults (density is highest in infancy and decreases as the scalp surface area expands during growth). High anagen to telogen ratio (typically >9:1 or >90% anagen). Normal follicular epithelium without perifollicular fibrosis or inflammation. No evidence of interface dermatitis or neoplastic changes. Subcutaneous tissue: Normal adipose tissue.

results.male_result: Normal skin architecture with intact epidermis showing regular stratification (stratum basale, spinosum, granulosum, and corneum). Epidermal thickness varies by age and site but generally shows normal maturation without atypia. Melanocytes are evenly distributed along the basal layer. Dermis shows intact collagen and elastic fibers, though collagen bundles may appear finer and less dense in younger children compared to adults. Adnexal structures are present; sebaceous glands are typically small and inconspicuous in prepubertal children. No inflammatory infiltrates, dysplasia, or neoplastic changes. Blood vessels and nerves appear normal.

results.male_result: The small intestinal mucosa displays normal villous architecture with tall, slender villi and a villus-to-crypt ratio of approximately 3:1 to 5:1. The surface epithelium is intact, consisting of absorptive enterocytes with a preserved brush border and interspersed goblet cells. There is no increase in intraepithelial lymphocytes (normal range is <25 lymphocytes per 100 enterocytes). The crypts of Lieberkühn are normal in size, depth, and distribution, showing no evidence of hyperplasia or atrophy. The lamina propria contains a normal, physiologic population of plasma cells, lymphocytes, and occasional eosinophils, with no acute inflammation, granulomas, or parasites observed.

results.male_result: Normal tissue architecture preserved; specific staining patterns depend on stain type used: PAS (Periodic Acid-Schiff) - glycogen and mucopolysaccharides stain magenta/pink in appropriate locations; Masson's Trichrome - collagen fibers stain blue/green, muscle fibers stain red, nuclei stain dark brown/black; Iron stain (Prussian Blue) - no abnormal iron deposits, minimal to no hemosiderin accumulation; Congo Red - no apple-green birefringence under polarized light indicating absence of amyloid deposits; Reticulin stain - normal reticulin fiber framework pattern; Alcian Blue - normal mucin distribution in appropriate tissues; no evidence of abnormal accumulations, deposits, or pathological organisms

results.male_result: Normal splenic architecture with well-defined white pulp (lymphoid follicles with germinal centers) and red pulp (splenic cords and sinusoids). No evidence of granulomas, neoplastic infiltration, or abnormal cell populations. Normal distribution of lymphocytes, macrophages, and reticular cells. No fibrosis, hemosiderin deposition, or extramedullary hematopoiesis.

results.male_result: Smear shows normal splenic cellular composition for age: predominant small mature lymphocytes reflecting active lymphoid follicles, scattered plasma cells, and macrophages. In infants and young children, occasional hematopoietic precursors (erythroid and myeloid) may be observed as a normal physiological finding (residual extramedullary hematopoiesis). No atypical cells, storage cells (such as Gaucher or Niemann-Pick cells), granulomas, or parasites (e.g., Leishmania) are identified.

results.male_result: Synovial membrane shows a thin intimal lining layer (1-3 cells thick) composed of type A (macrophage-like) and type B (fibroblast-like) synoviocytes. results.male_result: Seminiferous tubules are small in diameter, immature, and often appear as solid cords without a distinct central lumen. Germ cells consist exclusively of spermatogonia; there are no primary spermatocytes, spermatids, or mature spermatozoa (absence of active spermatogenesis). Sertoli cells are immature. The interstitium contains mesenchymal-like cells; mature Leydig cells are typically absent or unrecognizable (quiescent). No fibrosis, inflammation, or neoplasia.

results.female_result: Generally not applicable. Screening is not recommended for children. If performed for specific diagnostic indications: Negative for Intraepithelial Lesion or Malignancy (NILM). However, unlike the adult pattern, pre-pubertal results typically show an atrophic pattern (predominance of parabasal cells, lack of mature superficial/intermediate cells) due to low estrogen levels, which is normal for this developmental stage.

results.male_result: Benign thyroid tissue (Bethesda Category II): Adequate cellularity with benign follicular cells arranged in monolayered sheets and honeycomb pattern. Colloid present in background. No nuclear atypia, no nuclear grooves or pseudoinclusions. No atypical lymphocytes or malignant cells identified. Consistent with benign follicular nodule or nodular goiter.

results.male_result: Negative for acantholytic cells (Tzanck cells); no multinucleated giant cells observed; normal keratinocytes with intact cell morphology; no viral cytopathic changes; no evidence of pemphigus or herpetic infection
results.female_result: Negative for acantholytic cells (Tzanck cells); no multinucleated giant cells observed; normal keratinocytes with intact cell morphology; no viral cytopathic changes; no evidence of pemphigus or herpetic infection

results.female_result: Negative for Malignancy. The smear typically exhibits an atrophic pattern consistent with a prepubertal (hypoestrogenic) state. Microscopic examination reveals predominantly parabasal and small intermediate squamous epithelial cells. Superficial cells are absent or very rare. The background flora is often mixed or scanty; Lactobacillus (Döderlein bacilli) is typically absent due to the lack of glycogen in the epithelial cells.

results.male_result: Gut microbiome composition transitions rapidly during early childhood. By age 3, the community structure resembles the adult profile (Firmicutes and Bacteroidetes dominance), but often retains higher relative abundance of Actinobacteria (specifically Bifidobacterium) and facultative anaerobes compared to adults. Alpha diversity (Shannon index) increases with age, typically lower than adults in early childhood and reaching adult levels by adolescence. No pathogenic overgrowth detected.
results.female_result: Gut microbiome: Similar to males; composition transitions toward adult-like profile (Firmicutes/Bacteroidetes dominance) after age 3, with generally higher Bifidobacterium levels than adults. Diversity increases with age. Vaginal microbiome (Pre-pubertal): Distinct from reproductive-age adults. Typically scanty, mixed flora with high diversity and low bacterial load. NOT dominated by Lactobacillus. pH is neutral to alkaline (6.0-8.0). Common organisms include skin and enteric flora such as diphtheroids, coagulase-negative Staphylococci, and E. coli.

Symptom Association Probability (SAP): > 95% indicates positive association. Symptom Index (SI): > 50% indicates positive association.

results.female_result: Body Mass Index (BMI): Evaluated using age- and sex-specific growth chart percentiles (Healthy weight: 5th to <85th percentile); Body Fat Percentage: Age-dependent (typically 15-25% pre-puberty, naturally increases to 20-30% during puberty); Skeletal Muscle Mass: Increases with age but generally lower than males post-puberty; Basal Metabolic Rate (BMR): Highly variable based on age/weight, higher per kg than adults but lower absolute total; Total Body Water: Significantly higher in infants/young children (~70-75%) decreasing to adult levels (~50-55%) by adolescence.

results.male_result: Hydrogen Breath Test: Baseline <10 ppm, rise <20 ppm above baseline within 90-120 minutes; 13C-Urea Breath Test (H. pylori): Negative (Delta Over Baseline/DOB <4.0‰); Methane levels: <10 ppm at baseline.
results.female_result: Hydrogen Breath Test: Baseline <10 ppm, rise <20 ppm above baseline within 90-120 minutes; 13C-Urea Breath Test (H. pylori): Negative (Delta Over Baseline/DOB <4.0‰); Methane levels: <10 ppm at baseline.

results.male_result: Negative for exercise-induced ischemia and pathological arrhythmias (e.g., VT, SVT). Achieved target heart rate (≥85% of age-predicted maximum, typically 220 minus age). No significant ST-segment depression (≥1mm) or elevation. Normal blood pressure response (appropriate rise in systolic BP with intensity, diastolic stable or decreasing). No exercise-induced symptoms such as chest pain, dyspnea, pallor, or syncope. Exercise capacity (endurance time or METs) within normal percentiles for age and height (healthy male children typically achieve >10 METs). Normal heart rate recovery.
results.female_result: Negative for exercise-induced ischemia and pathological arrhythmias. Achieved target heart rate (≥85% of age-predicted maximum). No significant ST-segment depression (≥1mm) or elevation. Normal blood pressure response (appropriate rise in systolic BP with intensity). No exercise-induced symptoms such as chest pain, dyspnea, or syncope. Exercise capacity within normal percentiles for age and height (healthy female children typically achieve >9-10 METs, though capacity may plateau earlier than males during adolescence). Normal heart rate recovery.

results.male_result: Peak VO2: 40-55 mL/kg/min (values remain relatively stable or increase slightly with age in boys); Peak heart rate: 195-215 bpm (or >95% of predicted); O2 pulse: Increases linearly with height; VE/VCO2 slope: <32 (often slightly higher than adults but generally efficient); RER at peak: >1.00-1.05 (children may fatigue before reaching 1.10); Blood pressure: SBP increases with exercise (peak typically <180 mmHg depending on height), DBP remains stable; SpO2: >95%.
results.female_result: Peak VO2: 35-45 mL/kg/min (values may decrease relative to body weight during puberty due to increased adiposity); Peak heart rate: 195-215 bpm (or >95% of predicted); O2 pulse: Increases with height but lower than males post-puberty; VE/VCO2 slope: <32; RER at peak: >1.00-1.05; Blood pressure: SBP increases with exercise (peak typically <180 mmHg depending on height), DBP remains stable; SpO2: >95%.

results.male_result: Total colonic transit time: Mean approximately 29-36 hours (upper limit typically 62 hours). At 120 hours post-ingestion, less than 20% of radiopaque markers should be retained. Segmental transit times: Right colon ~9 hours, Left colon ~10 hours, Rectosigmoid ~10 hours. Transit is generally faster in infants and toddlers compared to older children.
results.female_result: Total colonic transit time: Mean approximately 29-36 hours (upper limit typically 62 hours). At 120 hours post-ingestion, less than 20% of radiopaque markers should be retained. Unlike adults, pre-pubertal females generally do not exhibit significantly longer transit times compared to males.

results.male_result: Normal trichromatic color vision. Able to distinguish primary colors (red, green, blue). For pre-literate children: Able to correctly identify shapes, symbols, or animals on pediatric pseudoisochromatic plates (e.g., Hardy-Rand-Rittler, Color Vision Testing Made Easy). For school-aged children: Able to correctly identify numbers on standard Ishihara plates (typically 12/14 or age-adjusted criteria).

For school-aged children: Able to correctly identify numbers on standard Ishihara plates (typically 12/14 or age-adjusted criteria).

results.male_result: Contrast sensitivity varies significantly by developmental stage. Infants: Very low sensitivity, limited to low spatial frequencies (detected via preferential looking). Preschoolers (3-5 years): Sensitivity is lower than adults, with Pelli-Robson scores typically ranging from 1.25 to 1.60 log units. School-age (6-12 years): Contrast sensitivity matures rapidly, typically reaching adult levels (Log CS 1.65-2.00; peak sensitivity at mid-spatial frequencies) by age 7-9. The curve shows the characteristic inverted U-shape, though high-spatial frequency sensitivity may be slightly reduced in younger children compared to adults.

Preschoolers (3-5 years): Sensitivity is lower than adults, with Pelli-Robson scores typically ranging from 1.25 to 1.60 log units. School-age (6-12 years): Contrast sensitivity matures rapidly, typically reaching adult levels (Log CS 1.65-2.00; peak sensitivity at mid-spatial frequencies) by age 7-9. The curve shows the characteristic inverted U-shape, though high-spatial frequency sensitivity may be slightly reduced in younger children compared to adults.

results.male_result: GH Stimulation Test (Arginine/Clonidine/Insulin/Glucagon): Peak GH >10 ng/mL is the standard cutoff to exclude GH deficiency (some protocols accept >7 ng/mL); ACTH Stimulation Test: Peak cortisol >18-20 mcg/dL (500-550 nmol/L); GnRH Stimulation Test: Pre-pubertal boys normally show a blunted response (LH peak <5 mIU/mL, typically FSH > LH); Pubertal boys show LH peak >5 mIU/mL and LH/FSH ratio >1.0; Water Deprivation Test: Urine osmolality >800 mOsm/kg (infants may show lower maximum concentration, approx >600 mOsm/kg).
results.female_result: GH Stimulation Test (Arginine/Clonidine/Insulin/Glucagon): Peak GH >10 ng/mL is the standard cutoff to exclude GH deficiency; ACTH Stimulation Test: Peak cortisol >18-20 mcg/dL (500-550 nmol/L); GnRH Stimulation Test: Pre-pubertal girls normally show a blunted LH response (LH peak <5 mIU/mL), though FSH response may be more exaggerated than in boys; Pubertal response (or Central Precocious Puberty) shows LH peak >5 mIU/mL; Water Deprivation Test: Urine osmolality >800 mOsm/kg (infants may show lower maximum concentration).

results.male_result: Generally not applicable. Standard adult erectile function metrics (IIEF-5 scores, NPT rigidity parameters, and pharmacologically induced erection standards) are not established or clinically used for children. While spontaneous reflexive erections are a normal physiological occurrence, they are not evaluated for sexual function.
results.female_result: Not applicable

results.male_result: Values are largely extrapolated from adult standards but interpreted based on age and size. Lower esophageal sphincter (LES) resting pressure: 10-45 mmHg; Integrated Relaxation Pressure (IRP): <15 mmHg (critical for excluding achalasia); Distal Contractile Integral (DCI): Generally 450-8000 mmHg·s·cm, but values may be lower in infants due to shorter esophageal length and potential sedation effects; Normal peristaltic propagation is expected.

results.male_result: For infants (< 6-7 months), high-frequency tympanometry (1000 Hz) is required, normally displaying a single peak. For older children, standard 226 Hz tympanometry typically shows a Type A tympanogram with peak compliance pressure between -150 and +50 daPa. Active function tests (inflation-deflation) and equilibration maneuvers (Toynbee or Valsalva) are often not feasible due to the child's inability to cooperate on command. Passive opening pressure may be lower than in adults due to cartilage flaccidity.
results.female_result: For infants (< 6-7 months), high-frequency tympanometry (1000 Hz) is required, normally displaying a single peak. Passive opening pressure may be lower than in adults due to cartilage flaccidity.

results.male_result: Negative for ischemia. Achieves target heart rate (usually ≥85% of predicted maximum; max HR in children is significantly higher, often near 195-205 bpm). Systolic BP rises appropriately with exercise (typically increases by 10-40 mmHg depending on stage, absolute peak correlates with body size), diastolic BP remains stable or decreases. No significant ST-segment depression or pathological elevation. No complex exercise-induced arrhythmias (isolated PVCs may decrease with exercise). Normal physical working capacity (PWC) or endurance time based on pediatric norms for the specific protocol (e.g., Bruce or Modified Bruce).
results.female_result: Negative for ischemia. Achieves target heart rate (usually ≥85% of predicted maximum; max HR significantly higher than adults). Systolic BP rises appropriately with exercise, diastolic BP remains stable or decreases. No significant ST-segment depression or pathological elevation. No complex exercise-induced arrhythmias. Normal physical working capacity or endurance time based on pediatric norms. Post-pubertal females may demonstrate slightly lower peak oxygen uptake (VO2 max) and endurance times compared to males.

results.male_result: Gait parameters are highly age-dependent and evolve towards maturity. Toddlers (1-3 years): Wide base of support, arms in 'high guard' position for balance (no reciprocal swing), foot-flat initial contact (lack of heel strike), short stride length, and high cadence (often >140 steps/min). Preschool/School-age (4-7 years): Base of support narrows, reciprocal arm swing develops, heel strike becomes defined, stride length increases with height, and cadence decreases. By age 7, gait kinematics generally resemble adult patterns but with smaller spatial parameters due to stature. Movement should be symmetrical without limping, circumduction, or persistent toe-walking.
results.female_result: Gait parameters are highly age-dependent and evolve towards maturity. Toddlers (1-3 years): Wide base of support, arms in 'high guard' position for balance (no reciprocal swing), foot-flat initial contact (lack of heel strike), short stride length, and high cadence (often >140 steps/min). Preschool/School-age (4-7 years): Base of support narrows, reciprocal arm swing develops, heel strike becomes defined, stride length increases with height, and cadence decreases. By age 7, gait kinematics generally resemble adult patterns but with smaller spatial parameters due to stature. Movement should be symmetrical without limping, circumduction, or persistent toe-walking.

results.male_result: Pre-pubertal (quiescent HPG axis): Baseline LH < 0.3 mIU/mL; Peak LH < 5.0 mIU/mL (commonly used cut-off is 3.3-5.0 mIU/mL depending on assay). The response is typically FSH-predominant (Peak LH / Peak FSH ratio < 0.6). A Peak LH > 5.0 mIU/mL indicates activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis (onset of puberty).
results.female_result: Pre-pubertal (quiescent HPG axis): Baseline LH < 0.3 mIU/mL; Peak LH < 5.0 mIU/mL. The response is typically FSH-predominant (Peak FSH > Peak LH). In Isolated Premature Thelarche, an exaggerated FSH response may occur, but Peak LH remains in the prepubertal range. A Peak LH > 5.0 mIU/mL indicates activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis (onset of puberty).

results.male_result: Heart Rate (HR): Newborn 100-160 bpm, Infant (1-12 mo) 100-150 bpm, Toddler (1-3 yr) 90-130 bpm, Preschool (3-5 yr) 80-120 bpm, School-age (6-12 yr) 70-110 bpm; Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP): Newborn 40-60 mmHg, Infant 50-70 mmHg, Child 60-90 mmHg; Central Venous Pressure (CVP): 2-6 mmHg (3-8 cmH2O); Pulmonary Artery Pressure (PAP): Mean 10-20 mmHg (higher in neonates: 15-25 mmHg); Pulmonary Capillary Wedge Pressure (PCWP): 5-12 mmHg; Cardiac Index (CI): 3.5-5.5 L/min/m²; Systemic Vascular Resistance Index (SVRI): 800-1600 dynes·sec·m²/cm⁵; Pulmonary Vascular Resistance Index (PVRI): 80-240 dynes·sec·m²/cm⁵; Stroke Volume Index (SVI): 30-60 mL/beat/m²; Mixed Venous Oxygen Saturation (SvO2): 70-75%

results.male_result: GnRH stimulation test: Pre-pubertal boys typically show a blunted response (LH peak < 5-6 mIU/mL, LH/FSH ratio < 0.6). A pubertal response (indicating Central Precocious Puberty or normal puberty) is defined by LH peak > 5-6 mIU/mL and LH/FSH ratio > 0.6. GH stimulation tests (Arginine, Clonidine, Insulin, Glucagon): Peak Growth Hormone level > 7-10 ng/mL (cutoff varies by assay/guideline) rules out GH deficiency. ACTH stimulation test: Cortisol peak > 18-20 μg/dL (500-550 nmol/L); specific attention is paid to 17-OHP response to rule out Congenital Adrenal Hyperplasia. Dexamethasone suppression: Serum cortisol < 1.8 μg/dL (50 nmol/L) following weight-adjusted low-dose administration.
results.female_result: GnRH stimulation test: Pre-pubertal girls often show an FSH-predominant response but with a low LH peak (< 5-6 mIU/mL). A pubertal response (indicating activation of the HPG axis) shows an LH peak > 5-6 mIU/mL and increased LH/FSH ratio. GH stimulation tests: Peak Growth Hormone level > 7-10 ng/mL rules out GH deficiency. ACTH stimulation test: Cortisol peak > 18-20 μg/dL (500-550 nmol/L); 17-OHP response used to screen for CAH. Dexamethasone suppression: Serum cortisol < 1.8 μg/dL following weight-adjusted low-dose administration. Clomiphene challenge test is not applicable for pediatric ovarian reserve evaluation.

results.male_result: Dosage: 0.5 mg/kg IV. For children > 1 year: ICG retention rate at 15 minutes (R15): < 10%; Plasma disappearance rate (PDR): 18-25% per minute (often higher/faster than adults due to metabolic rate). For neonates and infants: Clearance is significantly slower, and R15 is physiologically higher.
results.female_result: Dosage: 0.5 mg/kg IV. results.male_result: Schirmer Test I: Typically ≥20 mm wetting in 5 minutes (physiological tear production is higher in children); Tear Break-Up Time (TBUT): ≥10 seconds; Fluorescein Dye Disappearance Test (FDDT): Grade 0 or 1 (dye disappears from the tear meniscus within 5 minutes), preferred over Jones Test for drainage assessment.

results.male_result: Normal metabolic response to weight-adjusted standardized loading: Blood glucose returns to baseline within 2 hours post-glucose load (1.75 g/kg, max 75g; result <140 mg/dL or <7.8 mmol/L at 2 hours). Lactate clearance is efficient (peak lactate during maximal exercise is typically lower than adults, often 6-10 mmol/L, returning to baseline rapidly). Oxygen consumption (VO2 peak) appropriate for age, height, and weight. Respiratory Exchange Ratio (RER) >1.05-1.1 at maximal exertion indicates maximal effort. No evidence of pathological hypoglycemia or metabolic acidosis.

Oxygen consumption (VO2 peak) appropriate for age, height, and weight. Respiratory Exchange Ratio (RER) >1.05-1.1 at maximal exertion indicates maximal effort. results.male_result: Negative response: No significant increase in nasal symptoms (sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal obstruction, or pruritus/nose rubbing) following allergen challenge. In cooperative children, Nasal airway resistance remains stable with less than 60% increase from baseline, and Peak nasal inspiratory flow (PNIF) decrease is less than 40% from baseline. Acoustic rhinometry shows less than 30% reduction in minimal cross-sectional area (MCA). Symptom scores (using age-appropriate scales or observation) show no significant increase. No significant mucosal edema or secretion observed.

results.male_result: Negative response: No significant improvement in muscle strength following administration of neostigmine (dosage is strictly weight-based, typically 0.04 mg/kg IM). Baseline muscle strength remains unchanged. No clinical signs of cholinergic excess. Muscle power grading remains stable at baseline level (typically 5/5 in healthy children). Test is considered negative when there is no objective improvement in ptosis, diplopia, or limb weakness, as these symptoms are absent in healthy subjects.

results.male_result: Cognitive development is commensurate with chronological age. Performance on standardized pediatric assessment batteries (e.g., Bayley Scales, WPPSI, WISC, NEPSY) falls within the average range relative to age-matched peers. Global IQ and index scores (Verbal Comprehension, Visual Spatial, Fluid Reasoning, Working Memory, Processing Speed) typically fall within 1 standard deviation of the mean (Standard Scores 85-115, Scaled Scores 7-13, Percentile Ranks 16th-84th). Language acquisition, attention span, memory, and emerging executive functions are appropriate for the specific developmental stage. No evidence of global developmental delay, intellectual disability, specific learning disorders, or autism spectrum characteristics. Behavior and emotional regulation during testing are age-appropriate.

results.male_result: Extraocular movements should be full in all cardinal directions of gaze. By 4-6 months of age, ocular alignment should be stable (orthotropic) with no manifest strabismus (tropia). Smooth pursuit and saccadic movements are present but continue to mature in accuracy during early childhood; infants may show 'saccadic' pursuit. Convergence is typically robust, with a Near Point of Convergence (NPC) often closer than adults (< 6 cm). No pathological nystagmus observed. Corneal light reflex (Hirschberg test) should be centered.
results.female_result: Extraocular movements should be full in all cardinal directions of gaze. By 4-6 months of age, ocular alignment should be stable (orthotropic) with no manifest strabismus (tropia). Smooth pursuit and saccadic movements are present but continue to mature in accuracy during early childhood; infants may show 'saccadic' pursuit. Convergence is typically robust, with a Near Point of Convergence (NPC) often closer than adults (< 6 cm). No pathological nystagmus observed. Corneal light reflex (Hirschberg test) should be centered.

results.male_result: Normal olfactory function appropriate for developmental age. Infants and non-verbal toddlers demonstrate intact behavioral responses (e.g., facial grimacing, turning away, crying) to strong or noxious olfactory stimuli (e.g., alcohol, vinegar). Children aged >5 years correctly identify common, age-appropriate odorants (e.g., banana, chocolate, fish) on pediatric-validated smell identification tests (such as the Universal Sniff Test 'U-Sniff', Sniffin' Sticks 12-item screening, or Pediatric Smell Wheel) with accuracy scores falling within the normal percentile for their specific age group. No signs of congenital anosmia.

results.male_result: Highly age-dependent. Newborns: Axial Length (AL) ~17.0-17.5 mm, Corneal Curvature (K) ~50.0-52.0 D, Anterior Chamber Depth (ACD) ~2.2-2.8 mm. Age 2-3: AL increases to ~21.5-22.5 mm, K flattens to ~43.0-45.0 D. Age 6-14 (Emmetropic): AL ~22.5-23.5 mm, ACD ~3.0-3.6 mm. CCT stabilizes near adult values (520-560 μm) after age 1-2. In progressive myopia, AL may exceed 24.5 mm.
results.female_result: Highly age-dependent. Newborns: Axial Length (AL) ~16.5-17.0 mm, Corneal Curvature (K) ~50.5-52.5 D. Age 2-3: AL ~21.0-22.0 mm, K ~43.5-45.5 D. Age 6-14 (Emmetropic): AL ~22.0-23.0 mm. Girls generally exhibit slightly shorter AL and steeper K readings compared to boys of the same age. CCT values are similar to males.

results.male_result: Visual Acuity: Age-dependent (Infants: Fixate and follow; Age 3-4: 20/40 to 20/30; Age 5-6: approaches 20/20). Ocular Alignment: Orthotropia (straight eyes) is expected; transient instability is common in infants <4 months. Ocular Motility: Full range of motion in all gaze positions. Convergence: Near point of convergence (NPC) typically very close (<6-8 cm). Stereopsis: Present (demonstrated via Lang, Frisby, or Stereo Fly tests), reaching adult levels (≤40-60 seconds of arc) by school age.
results.female_result: Visual Acuity: Age-dependent (Infants: Fixate and follow; Age 3-4: 20/40 to 20/30; Age 5-6: approaches 20/20). Ocular Alignment: Orthotropia (straight eyes) is expected; transient instability is common in infants <4 months. Ocular Motility: Full range of motion in all gaze positions. Convergence: Near point of convergence (NPC) typically very close (<6-8 cm). Stereopsis: Present (demonstrated via Lang, Frisby, or Stereo Fly tests), reaching adult levels (≤40-60 seconds of arc) by school age.

results.male_result: Present and robust otoacoustic emissions bilaterally. In children, response amplitudes are typically significantly higher (often by 5-10 dB) than those observed in adults due to smaller ear canal volume and robust cochlear function. Transient evoked otoacoustic emissions (TEOAEs) demonstrate reproducibility ≥70% with signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) ≥6 dB across frequency bands. Distortion product otoacoustic emissions (DPOAEs) show SNR ≥6 dB at critical speech frequencies. Pass criteria met bilaterally, indicating intact outer hair cell function.

Distortion product otoacoustic emissions (DPOAEs) show SNR ≥6 dB at critical speech frequencies. Pass criteria met bilaterally, indicating intact outer hair cell function.

results.male_result: Minimal erythema dose (MED) within the age-appropriate range for the child's skin phototype; no abnormal or persistent erythema, edema, or vesiculation at 24 hours post-exposure; no immediate wheal or flare reaction to UVA or UVB exposure.

results.male_result: Pediatric posturography values are highly age-dependent and generally lower than adult values due to the developing neuromuscular system. 1. Composite Equilibrium Score: Increases with age; ranges are typically lower in early childhood (e.g., ages 4-6 may range 40-65) and approach adult values (≥70) by ages 12-15. 2. Sensory Organization Test (SOT): Conditions 1-3 (stable surface) mature earlier (approx. age 6-7). Conditions 4-6 (conflict conditions) mature later; specifically, Conditions 5 and 6 (vestibular reliance) show significantly lower scores in children under 10 compared to adults. 3. Center of Pressure (COP): Children exhibit greater postural sway amplitude and velocity than adults. 4. Weight distribution: Should be symmetrical (45-55% per limb), similar to adults.

Sensory Organization Test (SOT): Conditions 1-3 (stable surface) mature earlier (approx. age 6-7). Conditions 4-6 (conflict conditions) mature later; specifically, Conditions 5 and 6 (vestibular reliance) show significantly lower scores in children under 10 compared to adults. 3. Center of Pressure (COP): Children exhibit greater postural sway amplitude and velocity than adults. 4. Weight distribution: Should be symmetrical (45-55% per limb), similar to adults.

results.male_result: In children, results depend heavily on tooth development stage. For primary teeth and immature permanent teeth: A positive response to thermal tests (specifically cold) that subsides quickly is the standard indicator of vitality. Note that Electric Pulp Test (EPT) results are often unreliable and highly variable. Immature permanent teeth (with open apices) typically demonstrate a high threshold or no response to EPT even when vital, due to the incomplete development of the Plexus of Raschkow (nerve endings). Therefore, a lack of response to electrical stimulation is often a normal finding in developing teeth.

results.male_result: Total nasal airway resistance is significantly higher than in adults due to smaller nasal dimensions and decreases linearly with increasing age and height. Unilateral resistance is correspondingly higher. Total nasal airflow is generally lower than 500 cm³/s at 150 Pa in younger children.

results.male_result: Uroflowmetry: Bell-shaped curve implies normal function. Maximum flow rate (Qmax) is dependent on voided volume and age; generally Qmax >10 mL/s for age 4-6, and >15 mL/s for age 7-14. Cystometry: Expected Bladder Capacity (EBC) is calculated by formula: (Age in years + 1) × 30 mL (for children 2-12 years). Filling pressure remains low (<10-15 cmH2O). No uninhibited contractions. Pressure-Flow Study: Detrusor pressure at maximum flow (PdetQmax) is typically <60 cmH2O. Post-void residual (PVR) should be negligible (<20 mL or <10% of EBC). EMG: Quiet pelvic floor during voiding.
results.female_result: Uroflowmetry: Bell-shaped curve. Maximum flow rate (Qmax) increases with age and volume; typically >10-15 mL/s for school-age girls, approaching adult values in adolescence. Cystometry: Expected Bladder Capacity (EBC) = (Age in years + 1) × 30 mL. Filling phase shows stable detrusor with high compliance. Pressure-Flow Study: Voiding pressures are generally lower than males; PdetQmax typically <40 cmH2O. Post-void residual (PVR) <20 mL or <10% of EBC. EMG: Coordinated sphincter relaxation (silence) during voiding.

results.male_result: Rotary chair testing: VOR gain and phase lead are often higher in infants and young children compared to adults, normalizing towards adult values by adolescence. Vestibular Evoked Myogenic Potentials (VEMP): Present bilaterally; latencies are typically shorter than adults due to shorter neural pathways and smaller head/neck size (e.g., cVEMP p13: 11-15ms, n23: 19-23ms). Video Head Impulse Test (vHIT): VOR gain is generally maintained (0.8-1.2), though artifacts due to goggle fit are more common. VNG: Spontaneous nystagmus should be absent; smooth pursuit and saccadic accuracy mature with age (low gain or catch-up saccades in pursuit may be normal for children <5 years). Postural tests (Romberg/Fukuda): Stability is age-dependent; minor sway or inability to perform complex tasks (like Fukuda or tandem stand) is normal in children <6-7 years.

results.male_result: Results depend heavily on the child's age and ability to cooperate. Infants/Toddlers: Positive behavioral response (saccade or head turn) to peripheral stimuli (light or toy). Functional field may appear smaller than adults (e.g., 30-40 degrees temporally at birth, expanding rapidly). School-age children: Full visual field to confrontation testing. If automated perimetry is successful: Central and peripheral fields intact. Sensitivity values should be compared to age-specific normative data rather than adult norms. Reliability indices (fixation losses, false positives/negatives) may be higher than adult limits due to shorter attention spans.

results.male_result: Visual acuity and function are age-dependent. Newborns (0-2 mo): Fixate on faces/lights, follow large moving objects, estimated acuity ~20/400. Infants (3-12 mo): Reach for objects, steady fixation, acuity improves to ~20/50. Toddlers/Preschool (3-5 yrs): Acuity 20/40 to 20/30 using age-appropriate charts (LEA Symbols/HOTV). School age (6+ yrs): Should approach 20/20. Ocular alignment: Orthophoric (straight eyes); transient misalignment normal only up to 4 months. Stereopsis: Develops by 3-6 months, reaches adult levels by age 5-7. Pupillary reflexes: Equal, round, reactive to light (accommodation develops by 3-4 months).

Normal response shows progressive urine concentration while plasma osmolality remains stable (<295-300 mOsm/kg).

results.male_result: Not routinely applicable. If performed for suspected external genital lesions (e.g., condyloma), a normal result is Negative: No acetowhite changes observed on the genital skin or mucosa.

The classic transformation zone features seen in adults may not be fully developed or apparent.

results.male_result: Positive response: Localized erythema (flare) at injection site within 1-5 minutes, diameter approximately 10-30 mm, with surrounding wheal formation. No systemic symptoms observed.

results.male_result: Successful cannulation confirmed by selectivity index (adrenal vein cortisol/peripheral vein cortisol) ≥2:1 (unstimulated) or ≥3:1 (ACTH-stimulated). Normal bilateral adrenal function shows lateralization index (aldosterone/cortisol ratio of dominant side divided by non-dominant side) <4:1, indicating no unilateral aldosterone hypersecretion. Cortisol levels are used to confirm successful catheter placement in the adrenal veins. In healthy individuals, there is no evidence of lateralized aldosterone excess.

results.male_result: In healthy children, diagnostic nerve blocks are not routinely performed. When indicated for diagnostic purposes (e.g., complex regional pain syndrome, spasticity evaluation), the procedure often requires sedation or general anesthesia, making immediate verbal confirmation of sensory block difficult. Normal response: Temporary numbness or motor weakness in the targeted distribution consistent with the local anesthetic used. In sedated children, success is inferred by physiological stability and post-waking observation of reduced pain behaviors or improved range of motion (if motor block was intended). Strict adherence to weight-based dosing is required to ensure no signs of systemic toxicity.

results.female_result: Procedure is extremely rare in pediatric populations. If performed, normal findings depend on developmental stage: 1. Pre-pubertal: Uterus is small and infantile (approx. 2-4 cm depth); cervix is tight/stenotic; endometrium is thin and atrophic/inactive. 2. Post-pubertal/Adolescent: Uterine size approaches adult nulliparous dimensions (approx. 5-7 cm depth). results.male_result: Not applicable - Exploratory laparotomy is a diagnostic/therapeutic surgical procedure performed only when pathology is suspected. It is not a routine examination for healthy children. Normal intraoperative findings would include: liver smooth with normal color and consistency (organ is relatively larger in proportion to the abdominal cavity compared to adults), spleen normal in size, stomach and intestines with normal appearance and peristalsis, mesentery without pathological masses (benign reactive lymph nodes are more commonly visible in children than adults), peritoneum smooth and glistening without adhesions or fluid collections, retroperitoneum without masses, bladder normal in appearance (may be intra-abdominal rather than pelvic in infants/young children).

results.male_result: Not applicable
results.female_result: Normal fetal movement count: ≥10 movements per 12 hours, or ≥3-4 movements per hour during active periods. When using the 'Count to 10' method, fetal movements should reach 10 within 2 hours. Normal pattern shows increased activity after meals and in the evening, with periods of rest (fetal sleep cycles of 20-40 minutes). Movements should be distinct and perceptible, including kicks, rolls, and stretches. A sudden decrease of >50% from baseline or <10 movements in 12 hours warrants further evaluation.

results.male_result: Normal primary (deciduous) and young permanent teeth demonstrate uniform and high light transmission. No evidence of dental caries, developmental hypomineralization, or fractures. Interproximal contact areas show continuous light passage without interruption.

results.male_result: General assessment: Alert, active, cooperative (age-dependent), and displaying age-appropriate behavior. ASA Physical Status Classification: ASA I or II. Cardiovascular: No history of uncorrected congenital heart disease, normal heart rate and blood pressure for age, functional capacity indicated by ability to keep up with peers in play. Pulmonary: No signs of active Upper Respiratory Infection (URI) such as runny nose, cough, or fever in the past 2-4 weeks; SpO2 ≥95% on room air; asthma (if present) is well-controlled. Nutritional status: Weight and height within normal growth percentiles for age. No history of malignant hyperthermia or family history of anesthesia complications. NPO status confirmed according to pediatric guidelines (e.g., 2-4-6-8 rule). Informed consent obtained from parents/guardians.

results.male_result: HPLC is an analytical technique. Normal results depend on the specific analyte and the child's developmental stage. Common pediatric applications include: Hemoglobin variants (HbF is dominant in newborns [50-90%] and declines to adult levels [<2%] by 1-2 years old; HbA increases correspondingly); Hemoglobin A1c (HbA1c): 4.0-5.6% (generally similar to adults); Vitamin D (25-OH): 30-100 ng/mL; Catecholamines: Levels are age-dependent and reference ranges differ from adults; Amino acids: Strictly age-dependent ranges used for metabolic screening.
results.female_result: HPLC is an analytical technique. Normal results depend on the specific analyte and the child's developmental stage. results.male_result: Colony-forming units (CFU) are generally higher in children due to more active hematopoiesis. Approximate ranges per 10^5 mononuclear cells: CFU-GM: 60-250 colonies; CFU-E: 150-600 colonies; BFU-E: 30-100 colonies; CFU-GEMM: 5-25 colonies. Marrow cellularity is significantly higher (hypercellular) in infants and young children compared to adults.

results.male_result: Extremely low. In prepubertal boys (infants to ~10 years), PSA is typically undetectable or very low (< 0.1 ng/mL). During puberty, PSA levels rise gradually in correlation with increasing testosterone levels and prostate growth (Tanner stages). By late adolescence (15-19 years), values typically range from 0.1 to 2.0 ng/mL, which is still generally lower than the adult upper limit.
results.female_result: Not applicable - Females do not possess a prostate gland. This test is not clinically indicated for female children.

results.male_result: Generally not applicable for prepubertal children as the prostate is undeveloped and does not secrete fluid. For post-pubertal adolescents, results are similar to adults: Appearance: Thin, milky white fluid; pH: 6.2-6.8; Lecithin bodies: Abundant; WBC: <10/HPF.
results.female_result: Not applicable

results.male_result: Not applicable. Pre-pubertal children do not produce semen or ejaculate.

results.male_result: Epidermis: Normal stratified squamous epithelium; may appear thinner with less developed rete ridges compared to adults. Dermis: Normal collagen and elastic fiber architecture consistent with developmental age (collagen fibers may be finer); no inflammatory infiltrates, granulomas, or abnormal deposits. Adnexal structures: Hair follicles and eccrine glands appear normal. Sebaceous glands are typically small, indistinct, and quiescent (inactive) in prepubertal children. No fungal elements (hyphae, spores), parasites (mites, larvae), or pathogenic microorganisms identified. Melanocytes present in normal distribution.

results.item_name: Urinary and Serum Catecholamine Metabolites Test
results.male_result:
Values are highly age-dependent and frequently reported as a ratio to urinary creatinine (mg/g Cr) in children due to the difficulty of 24-hour collection. 

Approximate 24-hour Urine values:
- VMA: <1 yr: <1.5 mg/24h; 1-4 yr: <3.0 mg/24h; 5-9 yr: <4.0 mg/24h; >10 yr: 2-7 mg/24h.
- HVA: <1 yr: <3.0 mg/24h; 1-4 yr: <6.0 mg/24h; >5 yr: varies closer to adult range.

Random Urine (Spot) VMA/Creatinine Ratio (mg/g Cr): <1 yr: <22; 1-2 yr: <15; 2-4 yr: <10; 5-9 yr: <8; 10-14 yr: <5.
Random Urine (Spot) HVA/Creatinine Ratio (mg/g Cr): <1 yr: <32; 1-2 yr: <22; 2-4 yr: <16; 5-9 yr: <10; 10-14 yr: <8.

Plasma Free Normetanephrine: Neonates and infants have significantly higher upper reference limits compared to adults (can be >1.0 nmol/L in neonates).
